text: VATICAN CITY - In a surprise announcement Sunday, Pope Francis named five new cardinals, for Spain, El Salvador and three countries where Catholics are a tiny minority: Mali, Laos and Sweden."Their origin, from different parts of the world, manifests the universality of the Church spread out all over the Earth," Francis said, speaking from the window of the Apostolic Palace to thousands of faithful in St. Peter's Square.Those chosen are Monsignor Jean Zerbo, archbishop of Bamako, Mali; Monsignor Juan Jose Omella, archbishop of Barcelona, Spain; Monsignor Anders Arborelius, bishop of Stockholm; Monsignor Louis-Marie Ling Mangkhanekhoun, apostolic vicar of Pakse, Laos; and Monsignor Gregorio Rosa Chavez, an auxiliary bishop in San Salvador, El Salvador.Francis will formally elevate the churchmen to cardinal's rank in a ceremony at the Vatican on June 28. Then the new "princes of the church," as the red-hatted, elite corps of churchmen who elect popes are known, will co-celebrate Mass with Francis the next day, the Feast Day of Sts.

Recent terror attacks in Belgium have raised concerns about the country's security agencies and their ability to prevent such incidents. On November 4th, a man armed with a knife attacked a police officer in the city of Liege, injuring the officer before being shot dead. This incident follows a string of terror attacks in Belgium in recent years, including the 2016 Brussels bombings and the 2018 Liège shooting.Critics have pointed to failures in the Belgian security agencies, including missed warning signs and communication breakdowns. The country has been criticized for its lack of coordination between different agencies and for not sharing information effectively.Belgian officials have vowed to improve security measures and address any weaknesses in the system. Prime Minister Alexander De Croo has promised a full investigation into the recent attacks and has called for better cooperation between security agencies.The Belgian government is under pressure to demonstrate that it is taking the threat of terrorism seriously and to reassure the public that they are safe. However, many are skeptical about the government's ability to effectively prevent future attacks and protect its citizens.The recent terror attacks have once again brought the issue of security agencies' effectiveness and preparedness to the forefront in Belgium, prompting calls for urgent reforms and improvements.

KINSHASA - Congolese President Joseph Kabila on Friday rejected charges that his forces had violently broken up pro-democracy protests, using his first press conference in six years to accuse demonstrators of attacking police and arson.Congolese security forces have dealt with a spate of protests organized by the Catholic Church since the end of last year by firing tear gas, beating and sometimes shooting at protesters.Security forces shot dead at least six people and wounded dozens more last Sunday while breaking up a protest of churchgoers."I am not [and] ... will never be against peaceful protests by political parties," Kabila, wearing a dark suit and tie and sporting what has become his trademark goatee, told journalists in the presidential palace. "But [not] if a political demonstration is intended to burn, kill police ... incinerate things."The suppression of protests, triggered by Kabila's refusal to step down at the end of his mandate in December 2016, has prompted anger from clergy and civil rights groups and condemnation from Western powers.Scores of people have been killed in the capital Kinshasa. Elections meant to replace him have been repeatedly delayed, prompting his opponents to accuse him of scuppering the process in order to cling to power, a charge he has denied.Under a deal mediated by the church between Kabila and his opponents, the president was to step down at the end of last year, paving the way for an election early this year.

A new study has revealed a troubling connection between malnutrition and child mortality rates, with an estimated six million children worldwide dying each year due to lack of proper nutrition.The study, conducted by a team of researchers from several international organizations, analyzed data from various countries and found that malnutrition was a leading cause of death among children under the age of five. The majority of these deaths were attributed to illnesses such as pneumonia, diarrhea, and malaria, which are exacerbated by malnutrition.The findings underscore the urgent need for global action to address the root causes of malnutrition and improve access to nutritious food for children in low-income countries. The researchers emphasize that investing in nutrition interventions, such as breastfeeding promotion, vitamin supplementation, and food fortification, can significantly reduce child mortality rates and improve overall health outcomes.Experts are calling for increased funding and political commitment to ensure that all children have access to adequate nutrition and healthcare. Failure to address the issue of malnutrition, they warn, will continue to result in unnecessary deaths and hinder progress towards achieving global development goals.The study's authors hope that their findings will raise awareness about the devastating impact of malnutrition on child health and spur governments and international organizations to take immediate action to prevent needless deaths. By prioritizing nutrition and investing in proven interventions, we can save millions of lives and ensure a brighter future for the world's most vulnerable children.

Horse racing fans will have to wait at least another year for the chance to see a Triple Crown winner.Constant rain Saturday made conditions quite sloppy at the track. The mud and cool temperatures helped to throw Funny Cide off his stride as he ran against five other horses.Empire Maker was able to slice through puddles of standing water and claim the Belmont Stakes, the third jewel of horse racing's Triple Crown. Funny Cide placed third behind Ten Most Wanted.Empire maker and jockey Jerry Bailey covered the more than 2.4 kilometers in two minutes, 28 seconds. The run around the huge oval was the longest of the three races. Bailey said Empire Maker just kept going faster. "You could not believe the deafening roar of this crowd when we turned for home. And he threw his ears in the air and kind of went into second gear and waited for Ten Most Wanted. But then he kind of accelerated again," he said. "He is the best three-year-old I have ever been on and I think he will prove as the year goes on he is a really great horse in himself."

In a closely watched election, Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu appears to be on track for a fifth term in office. As votes continue to be counted, Netanyahu's Likud party has emerged as the frontrunner, with strong support from right-wing parties. Netanyahu, who has served as Prime Minister since 2009, is facing a tough challenge from his main rival, former military chief Benny Gantz. The outcome of the election is expected to have far-reaching implications for both Israeli domestic politics and the country's relations with the international community. Stay tuned for further updates as the final results come in.

In war-torn Congo, the cycle of violence against women continues as former girl soldiers who were raped during their time in militia groups are now facing rejection and stigma from their own communities. This backlash is pushing these vulnerable young women back into the arms of the very militias that once preyed on them.Many of these girls were forcibly recruited into armed groups, where they suffered unimaginable horrors including sexual violence. After managing to escape or being rescued from these militias, they find themselves shunned by their families and communities, who view them as tainted and undesirable.Without support or resources to reintegrate into society, these girls have few options for survival. The lack of access to education, jobs, and mental health services leaves them vulnerable to recruitment by armed groups, who promise protection and belonging in exchange for their loyalty.Human rights organizations and activists are calling for urgent intervention to break this harmful cycle. They are advocating for programs that provide psychosocial support, economic empowerment, and education for these girls to prevent them from falling back into the hands of militias.It is crucial for the international community, as well as the Congolese government, to prioritize the protection and rehabilitation of these vulnerable young women. The stigma and rejection they face must be addressed, and steps must be taken to ensure that they are not pushed back into the cycle of violence and exploitation that has plagued the region for far too long.

ADDIS ABABA - Several thousand Ethiopians demonstrated in the streets of the capital on Monday morning to protest ethnic-based attacks in the outskirts of the city in which more than 20 people died over the weekend. Commissioner of the federal police Zeynu Jemal said that five people were killed in the Monday protests and between 20 and 25 people were killed in the weekend attack. He said that 700 suspects are in custody. Victims of the attack and their families alleged the perpetrators are some groups of youths from the surrounding Oromia region. Police fired tear gas to try to disperse the demonstrators, but people continued to flock to central Meskel Square. The demonstrators are calling on the government to take firm measures against the perpetrators."We demand justice," some of the rally goers chanted when they passed by the offices of Prime Minister Abiy Ahmed, on the way to Meskel Square. "The government's task is to protect its citizens, not to send condolences after they are butchered," shouted a protester. There were also protests in Arba Mich, the capital city of Ethiopia's Southern region and the home area of many victims of Saturday's attacks, in which demonstrators kneeled to urge justice.

Leaders of the 10-member Association of Southeast Asian Nations - ASEAN - are gathered in Phnom Penh for a two-day summit that is expected to deal with simmering maritime disputes and a scheduled North Korean rocket launch.Host President Hun Sen focused on economic issues in his opening remarks Tuesday, urging the participating countries to work to narrow the gap between the region's richest and poorest countries."Within the region, although the development gap among ASEAN members has been noticeably narrow, it is still huge," he said. "This requires to double our efforts to promote further growth and improve equitable distribution of the fruits of growth at both the national and the regional among members countries."The official agenda stresses talks on ASEAN's goal of developing a European-style single market within the next three years. But several of the participants have acknowledged the leaders will not be able to avoid discussions on pressing security issues.The Philippines, Vietnam, Malaysia and Brunei all claim parts of the potentially resource-rich South China Sea, putting them at odds with Beijing, which claims all of the 3.5 million-square-kilometer waterway. The Philippines and Vietnam have both accused Chinese vessels of intruding into their exclusive economic zones and disrupting oil exploration activities.Manila has called for a summit of nations with claims on the South China Sea in order to reach an agreement on the use of the seas. Concern about North KoreaMeanwhile, several ASEAN members have raised concerns about North Korea's planned rocket launch this month.

The United Nations has recently released a report indicating that the increasing prices of energy are fueling a new "green gold rush" in the renewable energy sector. As traditional energy sources become more expensive and environmentally damaging, many countries and companies are turning to sustainable alternatives such as solar, wind, and hydro power.The report highlights the growing demand for renewable energy solutions as a key driver in the global shift towards a more sustainable future. With governments around the world committing to reducing their carbon emissions and investing in clean energy technologies, the renewable energy sector is seeing unprecedented growth and investment.Investors are flocking to renewable energy projects in hopes of capitalizing on the green economy boom. As the world transitions away from fossil fuels and towards cleaner energy sources, opportunities for financial gain in the renewable energy sector are abundant.The UN report also warns of the risks associated with the rapid expansion of the renewable energy market, including potential environmental and social impacts. It emphasizes the importance of ensuring that the transition to a greener economy is inclusive and equitable for all, particularly for communities that may be negatively affected by the shift away from traditional energy sources.Overall, the UN report underscores the urgent need for countries and companies to prioritize sustainable energy solutions in order to address the challenges of climate change and ensure a more sustainable future for all.

The Supreme Court in Brazil has announced that they will be postponing a decision on the future of President Jair Bolsonaro. The court was expected to rule on whether the President should be impeached for alleged corruption and mishandling of the COVID-19 pandemic. However, the court cited the need for more time to review all the evidence before making a final decision.This decision comes after weeks of protests across the country calling for Bolsonaro's impeachment. Many citizens have been critical of the President's handling of the pandemic, which has resulted in high numbers of cases and deaths in Brazil.

British Prime Minister Tony Blair says he will continue to urge that the United States and other industrialized nations cut so-called greenhouse gas emissions.After returning from talks with President Bush in Washington, Mr. Blair told British lawmakers Wednesday that international efforts to cut greenhouse gas emissions will not be effective unless the United States and other key economies participate.President Bush has expressed support for cutting emissions, but he contends that imposing strict limits would be harmful to U.S. businesses.Mr.

DAKAR - Stigma and rejection from their communities after returning home from armed groups are driving former girl soldiers in eastern Democratic Republic of Congo back into the militias, where they are prey to violence and sexual abuse, a charity said this week.For many girls in the conflict-ravaged east of the country, the pain of being spurned by their families and friends as survivors of rape was worse than the violence itself, according to a report by Britain-based Child Soldiers International (CSI)."It is better to die there than come home and be rejected," said one of the 150 former girl soldiers interviewed by CSI.Eastern Congo is plagued by dozens of armed groups that menace civilians and exploit mineral reserves. About a third of child soldiers are estimated to be girls, who are often married off to militants, abused and raped, activists say.While most girl soldiers in the vast central African nation are abducted by armed groups, others join voluntarily to obtain food and money, to seek protection against violence, or because their families cannot afford to pay their school fees, CSI says.Yet despite witnessing violence and suffering sexual abuse, some girls in militias do not want to return home for fear of shame and stigma, while many of those who do so end up rejoining the armed groups, said Sandra Olsson, program manager at CSI."It is shocking to see that stigma is one of the main reasons reintegration fails so badly for these girls," she told the Thomson Reuters Foundation by phone. "Even after being home for years, many still suffer from exclusion and discrimination.""We need to ensure these girls are accepted by their communities, rather than being frozen out," Olsson said.

French President Emmanuel Macron and Socialist candidate Anne Hidalgo have recently shared their economic tips for France's future during a public forum in Paris.Macron emphasized the importance of investing in innovation and technology to boost economic growth and create job opportunities. He also highlighted the need for structural reforms to make the French economy more competitive on the global stage.Hidalgo, on the other hand, focused on social equality and sustainability. She proposed increasing taxes on the wealthy to fund social programs and investing in renewable energy to address climate change.Both leaders agreed on the need for a strong and united Europe to tackle common challenges and promote economic recovery post-pandemic.As the French presidential election approaches, voters will have to consider these differing economic visions for the country's future.

Recent discoveries in several caves across Europe have sparked excitement among archaeologists and historians. The cave art found in these locations is believed to be tens of thousands of years old, leading some experts to suggest that it could have been created by Neanderthals.Neanderthals, a now-extinct species of hominids that lived in Europe and parts of Asia, were previously not thought to have the cognitive abilities necessary to create such intricate art. However, new research and findings have challenged this notion.The cave art includes detailed drawings of animals, geometric shapes, and handprints. The style and complexity of the art have led some researchers to speculate that it could indeed be the work of Neanderthals.If proven to be true, this discovery could significantly change our understanding of the capabilities and intelligence of Neanderthals. It could also shed new light on the development of human art and culture.Further research and analysis will be needed to confirm the origins of the cave art. Archaeologists and historians are eagerly awaiting the results, which could potentially rewrite the history books.

China has escalated tensions in the South China Sea by sending a second boat to a standoff with the Philippines. The Philippine Coast Guard reported that the Chinese vessel has been staying in the disputed waters despite repeated warnings to leave. The first boat, which arrived earlier this week, remains in the area as well. The Philippines has accused China of violating its sovereignty and maritime laws by refusing to retreat. The situation is being closely monitored by neighboring countries and international organizations as fears of a potential conflict continue to rise. China has yet to make a public statement regarding the standoff.

BAMAKO, MALI - Sufi Islam leaders in Mali have created a new collective to counter what they say is an influx of foreign-sponsored radical preachers and institutions courting Mali's young Muslims.Cherif Ousmane Madani Haidara, who leads prayers at the main mosque in Segou in central Mali, has been visiting a new mosque in a different city almost every Friday. Other Sufi leaders are doing the same. They call themselves the Collective of Muslim Spirtual Leaders, or GLSM, to use the French acronym.Cherif Ousmane Madani Haidara arrives at the celebration of his 61st birthday in Bamako. Among the guests are Sufi leaders from neighboring countries. Increased fears of radicalization in West Africa have many looking to the GLSM and the work Haidara and the group is doing in Mali.Haidara said there has been little oversight of religious organizations funded by foreign donors. These institutions, he said, are largely financed by money from Gulf states, and they have brought a new ideology to Mali.In Bamako, he said, radical preachers tell youth that killing nonbelievers will give them privileges in paradise — killing one person will give you one villa, killing two gives you two villas. He said that he and other Sufi leaders were instead telling the youth that killing is not in accordance with Islamic teaching.Aminata Diaby got the message.

In a thrilling finale last night, Eritrean musician Samira Abraha was crowned the winner of the year-long music contest, "Eritrean Idol". The competition, which showcased the talent of aspiring singers from across the country, came to a spectacular close as Abraha wowed the judges and audience with her powerful vocals and stage presence.Abraha, a 25-year-old from Asmara, has been singing since she was a young girl and has always dreamed of making a name for herself in the music industry.

According to a recent report from the United Nations, Lebanon is currently hosting over 1 million Syrian refugees. This makes Lebanon the country with the highest number of Syrian refugees per capita in the world.The influx of Syrian refugees into Lebanon has put a significant strain on the country's resources, including healthcare, education, and infrastructure. The Lebanese government and international organizations have been working together to provide assistance to the refugees, but the situation remains challenging.The UN has called for increased support from the international community to help alleviate the burden on Lebanon and ensure the well-being of the Syrian refugees. Despite the challenges, Lebanon has remained open to hosting those fleeing the conflict in Syria.The Syrian civil war, which began in 2011, has forced millions of people to flee their homes in search of safety. Many of these refugees have sought shelter in neighboring countries like Lebanon, where they face a new set of challenges as they rebuild their lives in a foreign land.

Israeli warplanes have attacked smuggling tunnels between the Gaza Strip and Egypt, after militants in Gaza fired at least two rockets into southern Israel. The tit-for-tat violence is further complicating prospects to draft a truce between Israel and the militant Islamic group Hamas. The rocket attacks have again become an almost everyday occurrence in the four weeks since Israel called off its 22-day offensive on militants in Gaza.Nearly a month after both sides declared separate cease-fires, efforts by Egypt to mediate a durable truce are deadlocked. Hamas wants Israel to open all of its border crossings, including one to Egypt. Israel wants Hamas to stop militants from firing rockets into its territory and the smuggling of weapons into the seaside enclave. Israeli officials say they will not consider reopening border crossings until Hamas returns Gilad Schalit, an Israeli soldier who has been held since he was captured in the Gaza Strip in 2006. Hamas legislator Mushir al-Masri, a spokesman for the militant Islamist group, rejected any attempt by Israel to link the release of Schalit to a longer-term cease-fire. Al-Masri says Hamas' position is obvious. He says Hamas wants a cease-fire and is not backing away on that issue. But he says the Israeli attempt to connect the Schalit case with a cease-fire agreement is going to destroy the process and he says Hamas considers that "a stab in the face" of the Egyptian efforts to mediate peace.

On September 13, the B.B. King Museum and Delta Interpretive Centre will open in the blues legend's hometown of Indianola, Mississippi. According to the museum website www.bbkingmuseum.org, "The B.B. King Museum and Delta Interpretive Centre will offer Delta young people hope. Proposed educational, cultural and character development programming will take the form of classes, mentoring, interactive exhibits that B.B. King could not find in the Mississippi of his youth. In addition, the stories of the Delta, from its history to its music, social mores, race relations, literature and legends, adversities and successes will be examined in one interpretive setting. Since all these elements came together to produce the Blues and Bluesmen, the B.B. King Museum and Delta Interpretive Centre will be a repository for the ingredients that brewed America's root music."Stevie Wonder to Receive Gershwin PrizeThe Library of Congress has chosen Motown legend Stevie Wonder as the second recipient of its Gershwin Prize for Popular Song. He'll be presented with the award on February 23, 2009 at the Library's Great Hall in Washington, D.C. The following night, Wonder will attend an all-star gala concert in his honor. Librarian of Congress James H. Billington say, "The Gershwin Prize was created to honor an artist whose creative output transcends distinctions between musical styles and idioms, bringing diverse listeners together, and fostering mutual understanding and appreciation.

As U.S. lawmakers and President Barack Obama haggle over plans to cut Washington's massive debt, the International Monetary Fund says the United States must take urgent and effective action on the issue. Monday, the newest IMF report on the U.S. economy said it is critical to the U.S. and other economies for Washington to promptly raise the legal limit on debt, while gradually cutting spending.Leaders of the U.S. Senate and House of Representatives are working on competing plans to raise the federal debt limit and trim budget deficits. Congress and the White House failed to reach a bipartisan agreement Sunday, prompting declines on many stock markets Monday. Gold prices also hit a record high. The leader of the majority party in the House, John Boehner, called on his fellow Republicans to support a short-term extension of the debt ceiling accompanied by a package of spending cuts. Boehner earlier said he was not sure a bipartisan deal could be reached.Senate Majority Leader Harry Reid, a Democrat, wants a longer-term deal on the debt ceiling, while also cutting trillions of dollars in spending. President Barack Obama has said he wants an ambitious debt-reduction package.August 2 is the deadline for raising the debt ceiling and allowing the federal government to continue to borrow money.

China's parliament, the National People's Congress, convenes in Beijing this week, and the country will complete its biggest shuffle of top leadership since the 1970's. The NPC is dismissed by some as a rubber-stamp institution that does the bidding of the ruling Communist Party, but others see it as a valuable conduit for bringing public opinion to senior leaders.The annual meeting of China's National People's Congress brings almost 3,000 delegates to the capital. In theory, the provinces, autonomous regions, municipalities and the armed forces elect delegates for five-year terms. In reality, the Communist Party decides who will be a delegate and those delegates never seriously challenge the party.The NPC acts as a mechanism through which the party hears the views of the enormously diverse population. As such, some view the NPC as China's rehearsal for real democracy."One can look at the NPC and find various ways in which it plays a role in helping information travel from the provinces, from society and up to the leadership, which some might see as a democratic process - but it clearly plays a role as a? channel for upward information," says David Zweig, a professor of Chinese politics at Hong Kong University of Science and Technology. China's constitution says state power belongs to the people, who exercise it through the NPC.At this year's NPC, which runs March 5 to the March 18, delegates will be asked to approve new laws senior leaders have already decided to pass. And they are expected to approve the successors to current rulers.The new Communist Party chairman and current vice president, Hu Jintao, is expected to replace President Jiang Zemin.

In a surprising turn of events at the Southeast Asian Games, Philippine boxers have forfeited their matches in various weight categories. This decision has shocked fans and officials alike, as the Philippine team was expected to perform well in the boxing competition.The reasons for the forfeits have not been officially disclosed by the Philippine team management. However, sources indicate that the decision may have been influenced by external factors, such as injuries or internal disputes within the team.The absence of the Philippine boxers has resulted in disappointment for both the athletes and their supporters. Many had high hopes for the boxers to bring home medals for the country, but those expectations have now been dashed.As the SEA Games continue, it remains to be seen how this development will impact the overall performance of the Philippine team. Fans are hopeful that the remaining athletes will be able to step up and make up for the losses in the boxing competition.

South Sudan's President Salva Kiir and rebel leader Riek Machar have signed a peace deal aimed at ending the five-year civil war in the country. The agreement, which was signed in the Ethiopian capital of Addis Ababa, will see the two leaders sharing power in a transitional government.The civil war in South Sudan has claimed the lives of tens of thousands of people and displaced millions since it broke out in 2013. The conflict has been characterized by ethnic violence and human rights abuses.Under the terms of the peace deal, President Kiir will remain as the head of state while Machar will be reinstated as the first vice president. The transitional government will be in place for a period of 36 months, after which elections will be held to determine the country's future leadership.Both leaders expressed optimism that the peace deal would bring an end to the violence and suffering in South Sudan. However, there are concerns about the implementation of the agreement and whether both sides will be able to uphold their commitments.The international community has welcomed the signing of the peace deal, with many calling it a significant step towards bringing lasting peace to South Sudan. The United Nations and African Union have pledged their support for the implementation of the agreement and the rebuilding of the war-torn country.It remains to be seen whether the peace deal will hold and whether it will bring about the stability and prosperity that the people of South Sudan have long been yearning for. But for now, there is hope that a new chapter of peace and reconciliation may be on the horizon.

In Ghana, the presidential candidate of the opposition United Front Party (UFP) has denied reports he has been removed as leader of the party ahead of the December general election. “We have about 51 members as our national executive and only three people are making this assertion. So, it is neither here nor there,” said Akwasi Addae. Addae insisted he is the party’s legitimate presidential candidate, despite what he calls are the attempts by a few disgruntled members to undermine his chances in the run-up to the polls. But, the national chairman of the UFP, Nana Agyenim Boateng, said the party’s national executives unanimously voted to remove its presidential candidate. Boateng said he has officially petitioned the Independent Electoral Commission (INEC) to remove the party’s candidate for the December poll. “I am presenting this to the electoral commission to have him removed. Article 6 of our constitution says that anybody that [undermines] the image of the party, such a person ceases to be a member of the party,” Boateng said. But, in defiance, Addae maintains that he will lead the party in the December vote. He said those seeking to remove him as presidential candidate for the vote are not being loyal to the party. Addae said the party’s future success would be undermined if senior officials force the group into a coalition with other parties in the run-up to the poll.

Renowned horror movie director Wes Craven has filed a lawsuit against his neighbor, comedian Pauly Shore, in a surprising turn of events. The lawsuit, which was filed in a Los Angeles court last week, alleges that Shore's constant loud parties and disruptive behavior have caused Craven and his family significant distress.According to the court documents, Craven claims that the noise from Shore's parties has made it impossible for him to work on his upcoming projects.

NAIROBI - Kenya has reported its first case of yellow fever in 25 years. The patient, who died while receiving treatment, is thought to have arrived five days ago from Angola, a country that is fighting to stop the outbreak of the disease.The patient was diagnosed with the virus at an advanced stage, according to Dr. Jackson Kioko, acting director of the Kenya Medical Service."We screened him, and we found out that he had a yellow fever virus and, of course, he presented himself to us in three advanced stages when he had a renal and liver failure,” Kioko said. “He succumbed because he presented himself to the facility too late."The yellow fever virus is transmitted by an infected mosquito and found in tropical regions of Africa and Latin America. Symptoms of yellow fever include headache, vomiting and fatigue.Statistics from Angolan health officials show that more than 50 people have recently died of the virus, and 250 are infected.The Angolan government has vaccinated half a million people against yellow fever, and health officials are targeting another million.Kenya is on high alert at its entry points, Kioko says."We've sent an alert notice to our all health workers to all hospitals, public and private, to ensure that they are on high alert,” Kioko said. Consequently, “all people who have traveled to high-risk countries where Ebola have been reported … [are] put on the list of suspicion and we are able to investigate them thoroughly."There is no specific treatment for yellow fever.

The United States has surpassed a grim milestone as the country's coronavirus death toll has now exceeded 250,000, according to data from Johns Hopkins University. This comes as new cases of the virus continue to rise sharply across the nation.The US has reported a total of over 11 million confirmed cases of COVID-19 since the start of the pandemic, with many states experiencing a surge in infections in recent weeks. Health experts are warning that the upcoming holiday season could further exacerbate the spread of the virus as people gather for Thanksgiving and other celebrations.Hospitals in several states are already reaching capacity, leading to concerns about the ability to provide adequate care for all patients. Officials are urging the public to adhere to safety guidelines such as wearing masks, practicing social distancing, and avoiding large gatherings to help slow the spread of the virus.The rising death toll and increasing number of cases serve as a stark reminder of the ongoing impact of the pandemic in the US. As the country continues to grapple with this public health crisis, it is crucial for individuals to take precautions to protect themselves and others from the virus.

In the 1970s, Carl Safina fished off the U.S. Atlantic coast for bluefin tuna, a majestic warm-blooded predator that travels at highway speeds and can weigh as much as 650 kilograms. "I've seen acres and acres of bluefin tuna at the surface, exploding through the surface and chasing prey fish." Safina says it wasn't a question of whether the fishermen would catch one. "When the fishing was good, we just assumed we would be going out and catching tuna."Over the last 40 years, though, the global adult bluefin tuna population has declined by more than 80 percent. The trend has been driven by industrial overfishing, coupled with surging demand, largely from Japan, which consumes 80 percent of the world's supply. The prized bluefin tuna is usually served as sashimi or sushi.In 1969, the International Commission for Conservation of Atlantic Tunas was charged with setting quotas and managing healthy tuna populations. They've failed in that mission, says Safina, now president of the conservation group, Blue Ocean Institute.When Carl Safina fished commercially off the U.S. Atlantic coast in the 1970s, blue fin tuna were abundant."In the last ten years, with the fish on the western side of the Atlantic pretty much demolished, a lot of the focus of the fishing has intensified on the eastern side of the Atlantic and the Mediterranean and that population is now falling apart," says Safina. "And the Tuna Commission has demonstrated on an annual basis its complete inability to control the fishing."

In a groundbreaking decision, the European Court has given the green light for the extradition of a US citizen. The individual, who is wanted in the United States for a series of serious crimes, had been fighting the extradition for several months. The European Court's ruling paves the way for the individual to be transferred to the US to face trial. This decision comes after a thorough review of the extradition request and legal arguments presented by both sides.The extradition is expected to take place in the coming weeks, pending any further legal challenges. The case has attracted significant attention due to the nature of the crimes the individual is accused of committing.The European Court's decision is seen as a victory for international cooperation in fighting crime and upholding the rule of law. The extradition process is a crucial mechanism for ensuring that individuals are held accountable for their actions, regardless of where they commit their crimes.The news of the European Court's ruling has been met with mixed reactions, with some praising the decision as a step towards justice, while others express concerns about the potential impact on the individual's rights.Overall, the decision by the European Court to allow the extradition of the US citizen marks a significant development in the ongoing fight against crime and the protection of the rule of law.

In Ethiopia, many children are facing severe malnutrition, putting them at risk of death. The country is currently experiencing a food crisis, exacerbated by factors such as drought and conflict.According to UNICEF, over 2.7 million children in Ethiopia are in need of treatment for severe acute malnutrition. This condition weakens the immune system, making children more vulnerable to diseases and infections.Without urgent intervention, these children face a high risk of death. Malnutrition not only affects their physical health but also their cognitive development, impacting their future prospects.Humanitarian organizations are calling for increased support and funding to address the food crisis in Ethiopia and prevent further loss of lives. Immediate action is crucial to save the lives of these vulnerable children and ensure their long-term well-being.

In a significant development, progress has been declared in the relations between Pakistan and Afghanistan. Following a meeting between Pakistani Prime Minister Imran Khan and Afghan President Ashraf Ghani, both leaders have agreed to work together to enhance bilateral ties and address mutual concerns.During the meeting, the two leaders discussed various issues including security cooperation, trade, and regional connectivity. They also expressed a shared commitment to promoting peace and stability in the region.This positive development comes at a crucial time as both countries continue to face challenges related to security and economic development. Improved relations between Pakistan and Afghanistan are expected to have a positive impact on the overall stability of the region.Moving forward, it is hoped that the recent progress in relations will lead to concrete steps being taken to strengthen cooperation and enhance mutual trust between the two neighboring countries.Observers are optimistic that this development will pave the way for further collaboration and contribute to peace and prosperity in the region.

U.S. Senator John Kerry and Susan Rice, the U.S. ambassador to the United Nations are reported to have been mentioned as possible candidates to head the World Bank.Indra Nooyi, the chairman and chief executive officer of soft drink maker PepsiCo, is also said to be on the short list of candidates compiled by the Obama administration to succeed Bank President Robert Zoellick. Zoellick is stepping down from the post when his term ends in June.A spokesperson for Kerry says he has not been contacted about the World Bank post, and would not be interested.Under an informal agreement dating back to World War II, the United States traditionally selects the president of the World Bank, while a European is picked to lead the International Monetary Fund. But the the world's emerging economies, especially the BRICS (Brazil, Russia, India, China and South Africa), have called for a more open process in order to identify a qualified candidate from a variety of nations.World Bank officials are taking nominations for Zoellick's successor until March 23. Other potential candidates include Lawrence Summers, President Barack Obama's former economic advisor and a former Treasury Secretary, and current U.S.

In a heartwarming display of international solidarity, Ethiopian children are receiving much-needed support from the Danish Society. The organization has stepped up to provide aid to vulnerable children in Ethiopia, who are facing a multitude of challenges including poverty, lack of access to education, and healthcare.The Danish Society's support includes the provision of essential items such as food, clothing, and school supplies. In addition, the organization is working to create sustainable solutions to help improve the lives of these children in the long term.This act of kindness from the Danish Society is a reminder of the power of global cooperation and solidarity in making a positive impact on the lives of those in need. It is a testament to the importance of reaching out and helping our fellow global citizens, especially in times of crisis.The children in Ethiopia who are receiving support from the Danish Society are surely grateful for this lifeline, and the impact of this assistance will be felt for years to come. It is a shining example of how individuals and organizations can come together to make a difference in the world.

The rise in piracy off the Somali coast has resulted in more global piracy cases in 2009 than in all of 2008, according to a maritime watchdog group. Despite the rise in attacks, the number of successful hijackings has fallen. The International Maritime Bureau of the International Chamber of Commerce, reports 306 piracy incidents have been reported in the first three quarters of 2009 compared to 293 reported in all of 2008. The group attributes the jumps in total attacks from last year to Somali pirates. Attacks in the Gulf of Aden have nearly doubled, and attacks east of the Somali coast have increased nearly four-fold. The piracy appears to be increasing in armed intensity as well. The group reports the number of attacks that involved the use of firearms has doubled from 2008. International Maritime Bureau Director Pottengal Mukundan sees a silver lining in the grim statistics. "What is very interesting is that the number of vessels, which have been successfully hijacked, [has] gone down from the figures last year," Mukundan said.Mukundan attributes the drop in hijackings both to the greater use of international navy patrols in the dangerous waters, as well as better prepared crews who brave the risky shipping lanes. He says captains of these ships are learning how to out-savvy the pirates in case of an attack, using evasive tactics that keep the ship moving even as the pirates open fire. Somalis claim organized piracy developed out of efforts by local fisherman to protect against illicit overfishing by outsiders taking advantage of the country's inner turmoil.

In recent years, citizen science has become a popular way for people to get involved in scientific research and contribute to important projects. Citizen science opportunities allow individuals to participate in research activities, collect data, and help scientists answer important questions.One of the key benefits of citizen science is that it allows non-scientists to make meaningful contributions to scientific research.

In a press conference today, former President George W. Bush called on Congress to ensure that funding for the troops in Iraq is secured without delay. The statement comes as tensions in the region continue to rise, with ongoing conflict and instability posing a threat to the safety and security of US military personnel.Bush emphasized the importance of providing the necessary resources and support for the troops on the ground, stressing that their mission is crucial to maintaining stability in the region and protecting American interests abroad. He also called for bipartisan cooperation in Congress to ensure that the funding is approved quickly and without any political delays.The former president's statement has drawn attention to the ongoing debate in Washington over military funding, with both Democrats and Republicans at odds over how to proceed. As the situation in Iraq remains volatile, it is crucial that Congress acts swiftly to ensure that the troops have the resources they need to carry out their mission effectively.

African leaders are expressing their hopes and expectations for the upcoming United Nations Climate Change Conference in Copenhagen. With the continent being one of the most vulnerable to the effects of climate change, there is a sense of urgency for concrete action to be taken.Many African countries have already taken steps to address climate change, such as implementing renewable energy sources and reducing carbon emissions.

Ukrainian anti-corruption activists have expressed their satisfaction with the recent conviction of former Trump campaign chairman Paul Manafort on financial crimes. Manafort, who was found guilty of tax evasion and bank fraud, had previously worked as a political consultant for pro-Russia Ukrainian politicians.The activists see Manafort's conviction as a step towards justice and accountability for corrupt practices that have plagued Ukraine's political landscape for years. They view it as a signal that those who engage in corrupt activities will be held accountable, regardless of their influence or connections.While some activists see Manafort's conviction as a victory of sorts, they also emphasize the need for continued efforts to root out corruption in Ukraine and hold all officials accountable for their actions. They believe that the fight against corruption is an ongoing struggle that requires constant vigilance and commitment.Overall, the conviction of Manafort has been met with cautious optimism by Ukrainian anti-corruption activists, who hope that it will serve as a deterrent to others who may be tempted to engage in corrupt practices. They see it as a small victory in the larger battle against corruption in Ukraine and a step towards a more transparent and accountable government.

In a heartwarming display of kindness and selflessness, twelve-year-old Alex Johnson has embarked on a mission to help homeless children in his community. Johnson, a resident of the city, decided to take action after seeing firsthand the struggles faced by children living on the streets.Armed with a backpack filled with essential items such as food, water, and hygiene products, Johnson sets out on foot every weekend to distribute these supplies to homeless children he encounters. His goal is to provide some comfort and relief to those in need, even if it is just for a brief moment."I just want to do something to help," Johnson said. "No child should have to go without basic necessities like food and clean water. I hope that my small actions can make a difference in their lives."Johnson's efforts have not gone unnoticed, with many members of the community praising his initiative and generosity. Local organizations have also expressed their support for his cause and have offered to assist him in his mission.As Johnson continues his walks to help homeless children, he hopes to inspire others to get involved and make a difference in their own communities. His dedication and compassion serve as a reminder that anyone, regardless of age, can have a positive impact on the world around them.

Joe Sakic scored on a power-play goal and a penalty shot within a 19-second span to lead the Colorado Avalanche to a 2-1 win over the New York Rangers Sunday night in National Hockey League action.Sakic was knocked down from behind by New York's Martin Straka after making his power play, leading to his penalty shot. He then squeezed a shot past goalie Kevin Weeks for the Avalanche's only scoring.Goalie Roberto Luongo made a season-high 53 saves to help the Florida Panthers defeat the Washington Capitals, 3-2. The victory was the first road win for the Panthers in nearly two months (since October 25). The Capitals set a team record with 55 shots on goal.Mike Modano and Jere Lehtinen each scored a goal and an assist for the Dallas Stars in their 5-3 win over the Chicago Blackhawks. Dallas has won seven of its last eight games.And finally, brothers Rob and Scott Niedermayer each scored a goal to lead the Anaheim Mighty Ducks to a 5-4 triumph over the San Jose Sharks.

Hafiz Mohammad Saeed founded the militant group Laskhar-e-Taiba. The United States has dubbed the group a terrorist organization. And earlier this week, Washington posted a $10 million bounty for information leading to Saeed's arrest and conviction. The Pakistani cleric, who operates openly in his country, responded by mocking the United States at a news conference, while Pakistani officials warned the U.S. to tread lightly.The 2008 terrorist bombing of an Indian hotel in Mumbai's financial district killed 166 people, including six Americans. The Pakistani-based militant group Lashkar-e-Taiba was allegedly behind the attack. Speaking publicly in Pakistan, Saeed says the money should go to him."To be honest, I am surprised that America doesn?t know where I am," he said. "Today I met the leadership of the Difa-e-Pakistan [Defense of Pakistan] Council here in Rawalpindi, and tomorrow I will be in Lahore and will release a schedule for the day after tomorrow, so America can contact me."With U.S.-Pakistani relations already strained, Pakistani officials aren?t pleased."I will say that it will impact negatively because this is an added pressure on Pakistan from the U.S. side and it seems to be having done it at the Indian behest," said Pakistani Lawmaker Mushahid Hussain. At issue now: whether Pakistan will re-open its ground route for NATO supplies into Afghanistan. Saeed has lobbied hard against this.Meantime, Pakistanis and others wonder: Why has the U.S. made this move now?Rand Corporation analyst Jonah Blank says the timing may have little significance."The decision may have been 'We are ready to present and there isn?t a pressing reason to hold off on presenting right now," he said.Other analysts have suggested that the U.S. is especially concerned about Saeed's group, which they say is definitively linked to many terrorist acts.

Relief workers continue to evacuate residents from hurricane-stricken regions of the U.S. Gulf states. Officials reported progress in their fifth day of rescue efforts, but many residents are frustrated with the pace of the relief work.Many of the evacuees are being taken to neighboring states. Nearly 100,000 have found shelter in Texas, while relief workers are bringing in beds for the displaced in Louisiana and Mississippi, the two states hardest hit.Air Force Colonel John Gomez is helping coordinate the military portion of the relief work. From Tyndall Air Force Base in Florida, Colonel Gomez told VOA the effort is massive. "We have something close to 300 helicopters, about 75 airlift aircraft, including our largest and most capable aircraft, the C-17 and C-5, as well as our airlift workhorse, the C-130, tanker aircraft, really every asset that the Air Force has available," he said.He said the aircraft carry supplies into the area and take evacuees out. The U.S. Navy has some 15 support ships in the region.Relief workers from private organizations are helping on the ground. A spokeswoman for the American Red Cross calls this the biggest response to a natural disaster in the organization's 125-year history. More than 3,000 Red Cross volunteers are in the area, along with thousands from other organizations.President Bush has ordered more than 7,000 active-duty troops into the region, to assist more than 20,000 National Guard troops on the ground and thousands more being deployed over the weekend. Friday, the president toured the area, then signed a $10.5 billion disaster aid package.Local officials in New Orleans have harshly criticized Washington for what they say has been a slow response to the disaster. In a radio address Saturday, Mr.

The Central African Republic (CAR) is currently facing a crisis as violence against Muslims has forced many to flee the country. This mass exodus of the Muslim population has not only created a humanitarian crisis but has also had a detrimental impact on the country's economy.The violence against Muslims in CAR has been ongoing for several years, with armed groups targeting Muslim communities and forcing them to leave their homes. Many have sought refuge in neighboring countries such as Chad and Cameroon, while others have been internally displaced within CAR.The departure of the Muslim population has had a significant impact on the country's economy. Muslims in CAR have traditionally been involved in various sectors of the economy, including trade and business. Their absence has disrupted supply chains and market dynamics, leading to shortages of goods and inflation.Furthermore, the departure of skilled laborers and entrepreneurs has hindered economic development and growth in CAR. The country's economy was already fragile, and the loss of the Muslim population has exacerbated the situation, leading to further economic hardship for the people of CAR.The international community has condemned the violence against Muslims in CAR and called for an end to the persecution and discrimination. Humanitarian organizations are working to provide aid and support to those affected by the crisis, but the situation remains dire.It is crucial for the government of CAR to address the root causes of the violence and work towards reconciliation and peacebuilding efforts to ensure the safe return of the Muslim population and restore stability to the country. Only then can CAR begin to rebuild its economy and move towards a more prosperous future for all its citizens.

WASHINGTON - Cambodian Commerce Minister Sun Chanthol has concluded a trade mission to the United States during which he emphasized his government’s willingness to make “deep” reforms to improve its investment climate.After visits to Los Angeles and Seattle, Minister Sun Chanthol capped off his week-long tour with an appearance in Washington, where he spoke about his government's recent anti-corruption efforts.“We carry out these reforms to help investors so that there will be less informal costs, less transportation costs, less energy costs, and [we will] train our people to have better skills," he said.He said the new-found political will to make reforms is due to last year’s general election -- in which the ruling Cambodian People’s Party lost a significant number of seats in parliament along with opposition allegations of vote fraud. Alex Feldman, president and CEO of the U.S.-ASEAN Business Council, says investment interest in Cambodia has grown steadily. But he said investors need more evidence of reform.“What the minister was talking about today was all very positive and all things that will attract business," he said. "We have seen progress. It’s probably not been as much as the U.S. investors or the government would like. Hopefully, this time we make more progress. But we’ll have to wait and see.”Feldman says Cambodia should continue to improve infrastructure, improve the skill base of its citizens and fight corruption. In 2013, Transparency International named Cambodia the most corrupt country in Southeast Asia. But Sun Chanthol says Phnom Penh has taken “unprecedented” steps toward reform, including passing an anti-corruption law and creating an anti-corruption unit.

WASHINGTON - Two new U.S. public opinion polls show Americans support President Donald Trump's missile attacks on Syria for its use of chemical weapons, but have little appetite for further military action against the regime of Syrian President Bashar al-Assad.A CBS News poll showed that by a 57-to-36 percent margin, those surveyed favored Trump's assault last week, when the U.S. launched 59 missiles at an airfield believed to have served as a base for Syria's gas attacks that killed dozens and sickened hundreds more.A Huffington Post/You Gov survey showed a 51-to-32 percent edge in favor of Trump's decision, the first direct U.S. attack on Syria during its six-year civil war.But both polls showed only about a fifth of those polled want the U.S. leader to take further military action against the Damascus government.The CBS poll said seven in 10 Americans think Trump should get congressional approval before taking any further action. The Huffington poll said that only a third of those it polled think that Trump's missile attack will deter Assad from using chemical weapons again.Trump's approval ratings were in the mid-to-upper 30 percent range before the attacks, historic lows for a new U.S. leader. But CBS said the figure has now risen to 43 percent, while a separate pollster, Gallup, said Trump's approval rating stands at 40 percent.

(October 5-19, 2012) International event encourages everyone to go outside, look skywards after dark, count the stars they see in certain constellations, and report what they see online™ International education and outreach program that builds public awareness and involvement in protecting water resources around the world by engaging citizens to conduct basic monitoring of their local water bodies. The program runs annually from March 22-Dec.

Israelis across the country observed Yom Kippur, the holiest day in Judaism, under tight security measures on Wednesday. The day of atonement was marked by fasting, prayer, and reflection for Jewish communities, with synagogues packed with worshipers. Due to security concerns, police were on high alert in Jerusalem and other major cities to ensure the safety of the public during the holiday. In recent years, Yom Kippur has been marred by incidents of violence, prompting authorities to increase security presence and implement strict measures to prevent any potential attacks.Despite the security precautions, many Israelis were able to participate in traditional prayers and rituals to mark the solemn occasion. Yom Kippur is a time for introspection and repentance, with many believers using the day to seek forgiveness and make amends for any wrongdoings.As the sun set on Wednesday evening, marking the end of Yom Kippur, Israelis gathered to break their fast and celebrate the completion of the holy day. The tight security measures put in place helped ensure that the observance of Yom Kippur proceeded smoothly and without incident.

The head of the U.S. Navy says the United States and Russia need to normalize their relations in the Baltics to avoid possible dangerous encounters between Russian planes and U.S. ships.Admiral John Richardson told reporters Monday at the Pentagon that recent close encounters in the Baltic increase the chance for what he calls a "tactical miscalculation." He said he hopes this kind of activity will stop."I don't think the Russians are trying to provoke an incident," Richardson said. "I think they're trying to send a signal. I think it's pretty clear that they are willing to let us know that they see that we are up there in the Baltics."A Russian warplane performed maneuvers above a U.S. jet flying over the Baltic Sea on Friday, weeks after Russian jets flew dangerously close to the warship USS Donald Cook off the coast of Kaliningrad.NATO has bolstered its military presence in Eastern Europe and the Baltics since Russia annexed Ukraine's Crimea in 2014.

In a tragic incident, the owner of a British soccer club and four others have been killed in a helicopter crash. The incident occurred near the club's stadium, with the helicopter reportedly bursting into flames upon impact. Emergency services quickly responded to the scene, but all five individuals onboard were pronounced dead.The soccer club released a statement expressing their shock and sadness at the loss of their owner, who was described as a dedicated and beloved figure within the organization. The cause of the crash is currently under investigation, with authorities working to determine the circumstances that led to the tragic incident.The news of the helicopter crash has sent shockwaves through the soccer community, with many expressing their condolences and support for the families of the victims. The club's next match has been postponed as a mark of respect for those who lost their lives in the crash.Details about the identities of the other four individuals onboard the helicopter have not been released at this time. The investigation into the crash is ongoing, and more information is expected to be revealed in the coming days.

DUBAI, UAE - Kasthuri Munirathinam, a 55-year-old mother of four from southern India, was plunged into debt after borrowing money to marry off her daughters. Like many before her, she was recruited through an agency and promised an opportunity to earn 1,000 Saudi riyals ($267) a month by working as a live-in maid in Saudi Arabia, the Arab region's largest economy where domestic help is in high demand.On September 29, just a few weeks after arriving in the kingdom, she tried to escape from her employer's house, using two of her saris to fashion a rope and climb from a third-floor window. What happened next is unclear and disputed. What is clear, however, is the price Munirathinam is paying for trying to leave. She lost most of her right arm and is now lying in a hospital bed in the Saudi capital of Riyadh.The incident highlights the precarious situation migrant workers across the Gulf face under a sponsorship system that ties their livelihood and residency visas to their employer. Rights researchers say domestic workers, who number at least 2.4 million across the Gulf, are among the most vulnerable because the majority are women and live with their sponsor. Labor laws in Gulf Arab nations often exclude domestic workers from their protections. Munirathinam's sisters, speaking by telephone to The Associated Press in India, say her employer was overworking her and not feeding her enough. In calls to her family, Munirathinam told relatives that when she complained to Saudi authorities about her treatment, her employer punished her by locking her in a room.According to her relatives, Munirathinam alleges that she lost her arm as a result of her employer's actions.

BEIJING - Amid a worsening tariff battle, China is putting off accepting license applications from American companies in financial services and other industries until Washington makes progress toward a settlement, an official of a business group said Tuesday.The disclosure is the first public confirmation of U.S. companies' fears that their operations in China or access to its markets might be disrupted by the battle over Beijing's technology policy. China is running out of American imports for penalties in response to U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff hikes, which has prompted worries that Chinese regulators might target operations of U.S. companies.The license delay applies to industries Beijing has promised to open to foreign competitors, according to Jacob Parker, vice president for China operations of the U.S.-China Business Council. The group represents some 200 American companies that do business with China.In meetings over the past three weeks, Cabinet-level officials told USCBC representatives they are putting off accepting applications "until the trajectory of the U.S.-China relationship improves and stabilizes," Parker said.Chinese authorities have promised to increase foreign access to areas including banking, securities, insurance and asset management."There seem to be domestic political pressures that are working against the perception of U.S. companies receiving benefits" during the dispute, Parker said.As for what improvement might entail, Parker said Chinese officials want an end to Trump's tariff hikes and a negotiated settlement.

The air industry is facing challenges due to the ongoing global pandemic, with travel restrictions and decreased demand causing major losses for airlines worldwide. However, industry experts are optimistic about the long-term prospects for the sector.Despite the current difficulties, many airlines are looking ahead and making strategic decisions to position themselves for future success. This includes investing in new technologies, updating fleet operations, and exploring new routes and partnerships.As countries slowly reopen their borders and travel begins to pick up again, airlines are hopeful that the industry will rebound in the coming years. The focus is on adapting to the new normal and finding ways to increase efficiency and profitability in a changing landscape.While short-term gains may be limited, the air industry remains resilient and innovative, with companies working towards sustainable growth in the long run. By taking a proactive approach and planning for the future, airlines are optimistic about the potential for recovery and success in the years to come.

Russian President Dmitry Medvedev ordered new restrictions on the sale of beer and similar beverages last month in an effort to battle Russia's rampant alcoholism. Questions are arising about why he is not targeting Russia's drink of choice, vodka.Russia is one of the world's largest per-capita consumers of alcohol. The average Russian drinks 18 liters of alcohol a year. That is more than twice the maximum amount considered healthy by the World Health Organization.The Lancet medical journal reported last month alcohol-related diseases caused around half of all deaths of Russians between the ages of 15 and 54.In an effort to battle Russia's rampant alcoholism, Russian President Dmitry Medvedev announced restrictions barring the sale of beer in cans or bottles larger than one-third of a liter. The new rules apply to ''light alcohol'' beverages, but not to wine, vodka or other hard liquors.The head of the Moscow-backed research group the New Eurasia Foundation, Andrei Kortunov, says it is no surprise Mr. Medvedev introduced the measure. "This is a tradition in Russia. That practically every new leader who comes to power in Moscow wants to do something about alcohol. Everyone understands that it is a national problem and something has to be done about that," he said.But some say his efforts fall short of addressing the real problem.Anna Boguslavskaya is in her 20s, lives in Moscow, and loves the nightlife. She says she goes to clubs and bars on a regular basis and thinks the restrictions are ineffective.

An aid group operating in the eastern part of the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) has raised concerns about the safety of their operations due to ongoing unrest in the region. The aid group, which provides crucial humanitarian assistance to vulnerable populations, cited escalating violence and insecurity as major obstacles to their work.The eastern DRC has been plagued by conflict and instability for years, with various armed groups carrying out attacks on civilians and displacing thousands of people. The situation has been further exacerbated by political tensions and power struggles, leading to a volatile environment that puts aid workers at risk.Despite the challenging circumstances, the aid group remains committed to helping those in need in the region. They are calling on all parties involved in the conflict to respect the neutrality of humanitarian organizations and ensure the safety of their staff and the populations they serve.The international community has also been urged to step up efforts to address the root causes of the conflict in the eastern DRC and support initiatives aimed at promoting peace and stability in the region. Without a concerted effort to address the underlying issues fueling the unrest, the situation is likely to deteriorate further, putting more lives at risk.The aid group's warning serves as a stark reminder of the dire humanitarian situation in the eastern DRC and the urgent need for action to protect vulnerable populations and aid workers in the region. It is imperative that all stakeholders work together to find lasting solutions to the conflict and create a safe environment for humanitarian operations to continue effectively.

JUBA - Operations have ground to a halt at South Sudan's largest hospital thanks to a week-long power outage, owing to a massive fuel shortage in the capital city.One doctor at Juba Teaching Hospital, speaking anonymously for fear of reprisals from his bosses, said doctors can no longer perform most emergency operations. The last time this happened — in January — 10 patients died because staff could not perform necessary procedures.The doctor said he tells patients to either buy fuel to run the hospital's generators for the duration of the surgery, or pay for a private clinic elsewhere.But this hospital serves the city's poorest residents. Most cannot afford it."Even me, they have been calling me now that we need operation, but I told them, frankly, there's nothing we can do, nothing in my hands," the doctor said.One woman in labor was writhing in pain in the maternity ward. She has suffered epileptic seizures.Helen Emmanuel lies in labor in need of a C-section in Juba Teaching Hospital, South Sudan's largest medical facility, May 23, 2016. The hospital has not had electricity for surgeries for a week. (J. Patinkin/VOA)Her mother-in-law, Jennifer Joseph, looked frantically for one jerry can — about twenty liters — worth of fuel so the woman could have an emergency C-section."They say that if the fuel is now ready, they will just make the operation now," Joseph said.But even if she finds the fuel, she doesn't know how she will pay for it."What can I say?

In a closely watched special election in Georgia, a Democrat has advanced to the next round in what many are calling a "Trump referendum". The election, held in a suburban district outside of Atlanta, has gained national attention as a potential indicator of the current political climate. The Democrat, who has not been named, will face off against a Republican in the final round of voting.The race has been framed by many as a test of support for former President Donald Trump and his brand of politics. Trump himself had endorsed the Republican candidate, calling on voters to reject the "radical left agenda" of the Democratic party. However, the Democrat's strong showing in the first round of voting indicates that the race is far from over.With control of Congress hanging in the balance, both parties are closely watching the developments in Georgia. The final round of voting is expected to be a tight race, with both candidates mobilizing their supporters for a fierce battle. The outcome of the election could have far-reaching implications for the future of politics in the state and across the country.

Three years have passed since a mud volcano erupted in Indonesia, displacing thousands of people and destroying homes and livelihoods. Despite the devastation caused by the disaster, many victims are still waiting for compensation from the government.The mud volcano, known as Lusi, erupted in 2006 in East Java, spewing hot mud and gas over nearby villages. The disaster buried homes, factories, and farmland, displacing more than 60,000 people. Since then, the affected communities have struggled to rebuild their lives and seek justice for their losses.While the Indonesian government has promised compensation for the victims, many have yet to receive any financial assistance. This has left families struggling to make ends meet and rebuild their homes. The slow progress in providing compensation has also sparked anger and frustration among the affected communities.Local activists and NGOs have been calling on the government to expedite the compensation process and ensure that all victims receive the help they need to recover from the disaster. They argue that the delays in providing assistance have only added to the suffering of those affected by the mud volcano eruption.As the victims of the Indonesian mud volcano continue to wait for compensation, many are hoping that the government will fulfill its promise and provide the support needed to help them rebuild their lives. Until then, the affected communities remain in limbo, uncertain of when they will receive the assistance they desperately need.

The state of Arizona finds itself deeply divided over its controversial immigration law as it heads to the Supreme Court for a pivotal decision. The law, known as SB 1070, was enacted in 2010 with the aim of cracking down on illegal immigration.Supporters of the law argue that it is necessary to protect the state's borders and uphold the rule of law. They believe that the federal government has not done enough to address the issue of illegal immigration, forcing states like Arizona to take matters into their own hands.However, opponents of SB 1070 argue that the law encourages racial profiling and discriminates against immigrants, both legal and illegal. They argue that it creates fear and distrust within immigrant communities and undermines the values of tolerance and diversity.The Supreme Court's decision on SB 1070 is expected to have far-reaching implications for immigration policy in the United States. It remains to be seen how the court will rule on this divisive issue, but one thing is certain: Arizona remains deeply divided over its immigration law.

A top United Nations official says drug traffickers are using a more sophisticated system to smuggle drugs from West Africa to Europe.Alexander Schmidt, the West African head of the U.N. Office on Drug Crime, says the amount of cocaine seized in western Africa has gone down, suggesting that smugglers are repositioning drug routes and becoming increasingly sophisticated at hiding their operations.Schmidt says officials also believe cartels are using lightweight submarines similar to ones seen off the South American and the Caribbean coasts.Schmidt says although a few Latin Americans still dominate the trade, West Africans are starting to have increasingly influential roles.He places the value of the drugs passing through West Africa at $800 million.Schmidt spoke Monday at a meeting of the policy committee of the West Africa Coast Initiative.

The leader of last month's coup in the Central African Republic (CAR) says he will serve as both president and defense minister.Michel Djotodia, whose rebel forces overthrew longtime President Francois Bozize, made the announcement in a decree read on state radio late Sunday.Members of Djotodia's Seleka rebel coalition will hold a number of other ministries. Nicolas Tiangaye -- an opposition leader who joined the previous government as part of a power-sharing deal -- remains as prime minister.The Central African Republic has experienced a series of coups since gaining independence from France in 1960.

In Mali, Sufi leaders have come together to preach against radicalization and promote peace in the region. Sufism, a mystical branch of Islam, emphasizes a spiritual connection with a higher power and advocates for tolerance and non-violence.The leaders have been speaking out against the rise of extremism in Mali, which has been plagued by conflict and instability in recent years. They have been urging their followers to reject radical ideologies and instead embrace the teachings of peace and love.The Sufi leaders believe that promoting a message of peace and unity is crucial in countering the influence of extremist groups in the region. They have been organizing events and gatherings to spread their message and educate people about the dangers of radicalization.Their efforts have been widely supported by the local community, who are eager to bring an end to the violence and unrest that has plagued Mali for so long. The Sufi leaders hope that their message will help to build a more peaceful and harmonious society in Mali, where people of all backgrounds can coexist peacefully.

In a recent statement, several senators have emphasized the United States' role as a leading advocate for human rights around the world. The group of senators highlighted the importance of standing up for human rights and promoting democracy globally.Senator Smith spoke passionately about the need for the U.S. to continue to champion human rights and hold other countries accountable for their actions. He stressed that America must lead by example and uphold its values, even in the face of adversity.Other senators echoed this sentiment, emphasizing the need for the U.S. to use its influence to protect vulnerable populations and support those fighting for their basic rights.The senators' remarks come at a critical time when human rights violations are on the rise in many parts of the world. They serve as a reminder of the important role that the United States plays in promoting and defending human rights on the international stage.

The United Nations has issued a warning to countries in the Asia-Pacific region, urging them to expedite their integration efforts in the face of potential risks from the Eurozone. With the ongoing economic challenges in Europe, the UN is concerned about the impact on the global economy, particularly in the Asia-Pacific region.The UN has emphasized the importance of regional cooperation and integration as a way to mitigate risks and enhance resilience to external shocks. By fostering closer economic ties and coordination among countries in the region, the Asia-Pacific can better navigate the uncertainties and challenges posed by the Eurozone.The call for accelerated integration comes at a critical time as the global economy continues to face uncertainty and volatility. The UN has urged countries in the Asia-Pacific to prioritize regional collaboration and strengthen institutions to ensure sustainable growth and development.As the Eurozone risks persist, the Asia-Pacific region must heed the UN's warning and take decisive action to bolster integration efforts. By working together and fostering closer economic ties, countries in the region can better weather the storm and build a more resilient and prosperous future.

Health officials in Nigeria, Africa's most populous nation, have urged Nigerians not to panic following the detection of the first case of the H1N1 virus, commonly known as swine flu, in the country. A nine-year old American girl in Lagos tested positive for swine flu infection. A wave of panic swept across the country over fears a swine flu pandemic may be in the offing in Nigeria. But Health Minister Babatunde Osotimehin moved quickly to assure Nigerians there are no other reported cases so far and that the government would ensure adequate protection. "Diagnostic facilities have been put in place so we can in-country diagnose the flu," said Osotimehin. "We also ensured that our epidemiologists at the level of the states have been trained to recognize, and commissioners have been sensitized to ensure that if they see cases within their states they will be able to recognize and deal with them effectively.
