They have drugs we have distributed to the states." The health minister said the girl, who had swine flu, had recovered after five days of treatment at a Lagos hospital. Nigeria placed its ports on high alert in the wake of the outbreak of swine flu in many countries earlier this year. Swine flu is a highly contagious disease which has flu-like symptoms such as fever, lethargy, lack of appetite and coughing.

The U.N. Humanitarian coordinator in Pakistan says the crisis for hundreds of thousands of displaced people who have returned to homes they fled in the Swat Valley and other tribal areas is not over. He says their needs as well as those of the population who remained in the area during fighting between the Pakistani government and Taliban militants continue to be acute. By June, nearly three million people had fled from the fighting in the tribal areas bordering Afghanistan. The United Nations reports more than 1.6 million of the displaced have now returned to the homes they fled in the Swat Valley and other areas the government considers safe. But, the U.N. Humanitarian Coordinator in Pakistan, Martin Mogwanja, says the crisis is far from over. He says military operations are continuing longer than expected. He says the sudden upsurges of fighting between Pakistani government troops and militant Taliban in different areas of return is prompting tens of thousands of people to once again flee their homes. He says this is causing further military operations and further displacement. "So, this kind of cyclical problem of displacement is extremely unsettling for the population and limits the possibility for the population to reinvest in economic activities such as agriculture, commerce and so on and so forth," he said.

Uganda Rebel Negotiator Under Fire For Allegedly Writing A Letter to President MuseveniA prominent rebel negotiator in Uganda is facing criticism and backlash after allegedly sending a letter to President Yoweri Museveni. The negotiator, who has been involved in peace talks between the Ugandan government and rebel groups, is accused of overstepping his role by directly contacting the president.The contents of the letter have not been disclosed, but the mere act of communicating with the president without proper authorization has raised concerns about the negotiator's impartiality and intentions. Many are questioning the negotiator's motives and whether he is truly committed to the peace process.President Museveni's office has not officially responded to the letter, but sources close to the government have stated that the president was surprised and disappointed by the negotiator's actions. The government has emphasized the need for all parties involved in the peace talks to adhere to established protocols and channels of communication.The rebel negotiator has not publicly commented on the issue, but it is clear that his actions have caused a significant stir in Uganda. Critics are calling for an investigation into the matter and for the negotiator to be held accountable for his actions.The peace talks in Uganda have been ongoing for several years, with hopes of bringing an end to the long-standing conflict between the government and various rebel groups. The recent incident involving the rebel negotiator writing a letter to President Museveni has highlighted the fragility of the peace process and the challenges that remain in achieving lasting peace in the region.

A new Tigrigna translation of a book on democracy has been released, making important political theory more accessible to Eritrean readers. The book, originally written in English, discusses the principles and practices of democracy and their importance in modern society. With the new translation, more people in Eritrea will be able to engage with these ideas and participate in conversations about governance and political systems. This effort to promote understanding and discussion of democracy is a positive step towards fostering a more informed and engaged citizenry.

The leader of the recent coup in the Central African Republic, General Antoine Kasaï, has solidified his hold on power in the country. After overthrowing the government last week, Kasaï has implemented a series of measures to consolidate his authority, including appointing loyalists to key government positions and suppressing any opposition.Despite facing condemnation from the international community, including the United Nations and neighboring countries, Kasaï shows no signs of backing down. Many fear that his actions could lead to further instability and violence in the already fragile nation.The situation in the Central African Republic remains fluid, with the population divided over Kasaï's rule.

French President Jacques Chirac and socialist presidential candidate Segolene Royal have offered sharply differing views of France's future.Mr. Chirac proposed corporate tax cuts, while Royal backed measures to ensure housing security.The president, a conservative who is not expected to seek a third term in April polls, says France must face up to rapid-growing economies in countries like China and India. He proposed cutting corporate taxes from 33 to 20 percent over the next five years. He also said companies that share profits equally between shareholders and employees could benefit from a lower tax rate of 10 percent.Royal used her New Year's greeting to address family issues, including the creation of a new fund to help people get off welfare and find jobs.

As the devastating war in Syria continues to ravage the country and its people, the United Nations and the European Union are coming together in a joint effort to revive the stalled peace process.The conflict in Syria has been ongoing for over a decade now, with various parties involved and countless lives lost. The humanitarian situation in the country is dire, with millions of people displaced and in need of urgent assistance.The UN-EU conference, set to take place in the coming weeks, will bring together key stakeholders from both organizations as well as representatives from the Syrian government and opposition groups. The goal of the conference is to reinvigorate the peace process and work towards a lasting political solution to the conflict.Both the UN and the EU have stressed the importance of a political resolution to the conflict in Syria, highlighting the need for all parties to come together at the negotiating table and work towards a peaceful settlement.While the road to peace in Syria may be long and arduous, the UN-EU conference offers a glimmer of hope for the war-torn country and its beleaguered population. It is crucial that all parties involved approach the negotiations with a spirit of cooperation and compromise in order to finally bring an end to the suffering in Syria.

More than a thousand people from some 50 nations are in Houston, this week, for the 25th World Ports Conference, sponsored by the International Association of Ports and Harbors. As VOA's Greg Flakus reports from Houston, conference participants are discussing ways of meeting the challenge of an unprecedented increase in world trade that puts a strain on ports.The expansion of commerce, worldwide, relies on a shipping industry to move commodities and products from port to port. But keeping up with demand for ships and harbor facilities can be a daunting task. Just consider this fact presented in one of the World Ports Conference panels: The World Bank predicted that, between 2000 and 2010, the world economy would grow 33 percent - from $30 trillion-a -year to $40 trillion. But the world economy surged past $41 trillion-a-year in 2004.Such explosive growth places a large burden on those who are responsible for maintaining, operating and expanding ports, worldwide. One of them is Thomas Kornegay, executive director of the Port of Houston and president of the IAPH. He says this conference has allowed him to compare notes on the world trade outlook with his counterparts from around the globe."We do talk about the increase in trade and how it is happening and what their estimates are and what our estimates are, so that we can all try to get a handle on what is really going to happen," he said. "Because we are all guessing, right? Nobody knows the future, we just try to predict what it might be."Kornegay says protecting ports and shipments from terrorists and criminals has been costly, but that ports have managed to improve security without impeding the flow of commerce.

ALGIERS, ALGERIA - Algerian teenagers and lawyers held protests Monday, and workers held scattered walkouts, as their tense nation waits to see whether ailing President Abdelaziz Bouteflika offers any concessions in the face of unprecedented protests.Algerian media and protest leaders expect it could be a decisive day for the gas-rich North African country, after Bouteflika returned home Sunday from two weeks in a Swiss hospital.His absence saw growing demonstrations demanding that he withdraw his candidacy for a fifth term in next month's election. Algerians have hardly seen Bouteflika since he suffered a stroke in 2013, and anger has mounted at the country's secretive power structure.Security was high Monday in Algiers, where some businesses were shuttered by a second day of strikes. Other stores and administrative offices remained open.Middle school and high school students held protests in several towns, according to local media. Education Minister Nouria Bengahbrit appealed on her Facebook page for protesters to "leave schools out of political turbulence'' shaking the country.Meanwhile, lawyers in their black robes gathered Monday in front of courthouses to join calls for Bouteflika to abandon his bid for another term.Exceptionally, some judges also joined a lawyers' protest in the city of Bedjaia in Kabylie, a region historically opposed to the powers-that-be in Algiers. Judges are normally banned from publicly demonstrating, like police and soldiers.The unprecedented citizens' revolt began last month and has drawn millions into the streets of cities across the country to say no to a fifth term for their 82-year-old president — and no to a system blamed for corruption and keeping Bouteflika in office despite his ailments.A wily political survivor, Bouteflika fought in Algeria's independence war against French forces and has played a role in Algeria's major developments for the past half-century.

ISLAMABAD - Afghan officials appear confident a planned deployment of about 300 U.S. Marines will help local forces reverse insurgent gains in the embattled southern province of Helmand.Backed by airpower, the Afghan National Army has intensified offensive operations in the largest Afghan poppy-growing province, after the Taliban captured the strategically important district center of Sangin last week, although government officials continue to dispute the claim.Afghan forces in overnight operations are reported to have killed dozens of insurgents and destroyed several narcotics-producing factories in Helmand.The provincial governor, Hayatullah Hayat, says national security forces are prepared, and better placed this year to beat back the Taliban. They already have cleared areas around the provincial capital of Lashkargah and nearby districts.“We have [also] started clearing pockets of [insurgents] in Garmsir district, in Marjah district, and also this will be done in Sangin district,” Hayat told VOA.Hayat sounded upbeat about a planned deployment of Marines in Helmand, saying it will boost local efforts to evict the Taliban, which is currently in control of most of the province.“I am quite sure they will have definitely lots of positives to bring in the frontline and also changing the security situation down in Helmand,” Hayat noted. He emphasized that Afghans will continue to lead the security operations, and U.S.

ISLAMABAD - Afghan President Ashraf Ghani says his meetings with Pakistani leaders in Islamabad have made significant progress towards developing close economic relations and strengthening joint efforts against the common threat of terrorism. Mutual suspicions, mistrust and uncertainty have been the hallmark of relations between Pakistan and Afghanistan, which share a long but mostly porous border. Their ties plunged to new lows toward the end of former President Hamid Karzai’s term in Kabul when he regularly accused the Pakistani military of supporting the Taliban insurgency in Afghanistan, charges Islamabad denied. However, officials in both countries have reported a reduction in tensions since Asharf Ghani was sworn in as the new Afghan president in September. Ghani acknowledged the progress Saturday at the conclusion of his two-day visit with civilian and military leaders in Islamabad. He was addressing a news conference with Pakistani Prime Minister Nawaz Sharif. President Ghani said that the two countries have begun a comprehensive dialogue on strengthening security cooperation against the common threat of terrorism. “We also discussed that we must overcome the past. We will not permit the past to destroy the future… The most fundamental part, Mr. Prime Minister for which I thank you, is that our fates are linked because terror knows no boundaries," saud Ghani.

A study on two Vietnamese women who died earlier this year from bird flu indicates that resistance to the antiviral drug Tamiflu might be more common than thought. The patients died despite receiving recommended doses of the drug very early in their infections.The study by doctors in Ho Chi Minh City raises concern because Tamiflu is the main drug that nations are stockpiling to defend themselves against the virulent H5N1 strain of avian flu. So far, it has killed more than 70 people in South Asia since 2003.In the study, doctors found that two people who died of bird flu, a Vietnamese mother and her 13-year-old daughter were the victims of a form of the virus that resisted treatment with full doses of Tamiflu given to them only one day after becoming infected."Tamiflu may not be adequate in at least a proportion of patients," said Dr. Menno de Jong at the Ho Chi Minh City unit at the Hospital for Tropical Diseases in London.He is the lead author of the study appearing in the New England Journal of Medicine."Resistance to the drug may not occur infrequently to H5N1 influenza since we detected the resistant virus in two of seven patients who we followed throughout the treatment period," he added.Dr. de Jong and his colleagues say the findings suggest the need for stronger doses of Tamiflu in some bird flu patients or additional antiviral drugs.In New York City, pediatrician Anna Moscona of the Weill-Cornell Medical College agrees."

NIAMEY, NIGER - Niger's opposition coalition said Tuesday that its candidate, Hama Amadou, would not contest a runoff election March 20, increasing the chances that President Mahamadou Issoufou will win a second term.Amadou has been in prison since November on charges relating to baby-trafficking. He says he is innocent and a victim of political repression. The government denies wrongdoing and says it follows the law."

President Trump's recent executive order rolling back key environmental protections has drawn criticism from both climate activists and skeptics.The order, signed on Tuesday, aims to dismantle the Obama-era Clean Power Plan, which aimed to reduce greenhouse gas emissions from power plants. Trump's administration argues that the regulation was too restrictive on the coal industry and hindered economic growth.However, environmental activists have lambasted the move, calling it a step backwards in the fight against climate change. They argue that the Clean Power Plan was a crucial step towards reducing emissions and transitioning to renewable energy sources.Climate skeptics, on the other hand, have also expressed their dissatisfaction with the executive order, but for different reasons. Some believe that the government should not be involved in regulating greenhouse gas emissions, while others argue that the plan did not go far enough in protecting the environment.The decision to repeal the Clean Power Plan has sparked a heated debate between those who believe in the urgency of addressing climate change and those who prioritize economic interests. The implications of Trump's order remain to be seen, but it is clear that it has reignited the ongoing discussion about the environment and the role of government in regulating emissions.

The newly appointed Saudi Ambassador to the United States, Prince Khalid bin Salman, emphasized in a recent interview that Saudi Arabia is actively engaged in the fight against terrorism. In his first public comments since being named ambassador, Prince Khalid highlighted the kingdom's ongoing efforts to combat extremism and promote peace and stability in the region."We are committed to working closely with our allies, including the United States, to combat terrorism and extremism in all its forms," Prince Khalid stated. He also praised the strong partnership between Saudi Arabia and the US in addressing common security challenges and promoting regional stability.Saudi Arabia has been a key ally in the global fight against terrorism, with the kingdom actively participating in multinational efforts to combat extremist groups such as ISIS and Al-Qaeda. The country has also taken significant steps to counter the spread of extremist ideologies and promote moderate Islam.Prince Khalid's comments come at a time when the international community is increasingly focused on addressing the threat of terrorism and extremism. As Saudi Arabia continues to play a leading role in these efforts, the kingdom's partnership with the US is expected to strengthen even further in the coming years.

CAPITOL HILL - U.S. senators of both political parties piled on in defense of America’s role as a human rights advocate, one week after Secretary of State Rex Tillerson suggested that a values based outreach to the world hinders U.S. national security goals.“America’s strength is in our ideals, our values: speaking up for democracy, speaking up for human rights, anti-corruption and embracing diversity,” said Democratic Senator Ben Cardin of Maryland.“So many of the groups around the world who are fighting for the principles that we as a nation stand for – democracy, a free press, freedom of religion, freedom of expression – they look to America for inspiration,” said Republican Senator Marco Rubio of Florida.Both lawmakers spoke Tuesday at a hearing of the Senate Foreign Relations Committee, one day after Republican Senator John McCain of Arizona penned an opinion piece in The New York Times newspaper in which he accused Tillerson of turning his back on decades of American human rights advocacy on the world stage.Secretary of State Rex Tillerson delivers remarks at the U.S. Department of State’s Foreign Affairs Day Memorial Plaque Ceremony at the Department in Washington, D.C., May 5, 2017.Addressing State Department employees last week, Tillerson was quoted as saying that “in some circumstances, if you condition our national security efforts on someone adopting our values, we probably can’t achieve our national security goals.”“With those words, Secretary Tillerson sent a message to oppressed people everywhere: Don’t look to the United States for hope,” McCain wrote. “[I]f you happen to be in the way of our forging relationships with your oppressors that could serve our security and economic interests, good luck to you. You’re on your own.”McCain wasn’t alone in his criticism.“

Several Philippine boxers have forfeited their matches in gold medal bouts to protest what they called biased judging at the Southeast Asian Games in Nakhon Ratchasima, Thailand.The decision whether or not to fight was left to each boxer. Two elected to forfeit at the opening bell and four more during their bouts.One Filipino fighter, Larry Semillano, fought his bout but lost to Thai Olympic champion Manus Boonjamnong. The forfeits contributed to a Thai sweep of all 10 boxing gold medals.In other events, Malaysia defeated Thailand in women's basketball, 61-60 in the last round-robin match to take the gold. The Philippines won the men's basketball title with a 94-53 victory over Thailand.Malaysia won both field hockey finals taking the men's event over Singapore, 7-0, and the women's title over Thailand, 6-0. The Philippines won both softball gold medals, with the men's team beating Indonesia, 11-2, and the women's squad downing Singapore, 7-0.Thailand won two golds in judo, while Malaysia and Vietnam took three golds each in karate.

In the upcoming election for new members of the UN Human Rights Council, concerns have been raised about the candidates running for a seat on the prestigious body. The Human Rights Council is responsible for promoting and protecting human rights around the world, making it a critical organization within the United Nations.Some of the candidates have been criticized for their human rights records in their own countries, with allegations of violations and abuses being brought to light. This has raised doubts about their suitability to serve on the council and uphold the values it stands for.Human rights advocates are urging the UN to carefully consider the candidates and their backgrounds before making a decision. They emphasize the importance of electing members who have a proven track record of promoting and protecting human rights, as well as holding perpetrators of violations accountable.The UN Human Rights Council plays a crucial role in addressing human rights issues globally, and it is essential that its members are committed to upholding the highest standards of human rights. As the election approaches, the international community will be closely watching to see which candidates are ultimately chosen to serve on the council.

China's Three Gorges Dam, the world's largest hydropower project, is facing criticism from Chinese officials who warn that the dam could become an environmental "catastrophe." The dam, which was completed in 2006, has been praised for its ability to generate clean energy and control flooding along the Yangtze River. However, concerns have been raised about the dam's impact on the environment, including its effect on water quality, biodiversity, and erosion.Chinese environmental officials have expressed concerns that the dam's reservoir is causing significant ecological damage, including the loss of biodiversity and the deterioration of water quality. The dam has also been blamed for causing erosion along the Yangtze River, leading to increased sedimentation and potential risks for downstream communities.Despite these warnings, the Chinese government has defended the dam as a crucial source of clean energy and a key tool for flood control. The dam has been a significant source of hydropower for China, generating electricity for millions of people. However, critics argue that the environmental costs of the dam may outweigh its benefits.The debate over the Three Gorges Dam highlights the complex relationship between development, energy production, and environmental conservation in China. As the Chinese government weighs the costs and benefits of the dam, it will be crucial to consider the long-term environmental impacts and potential risks associated with this massive infrastructure project.

In a recent speech, President Biden warned that Iran's leaders are "sowing seeds of their own destruction" by continuing to support terrorism and pursue nuclear weapons. The comments come as tensions between the United States and Iran have escalated in recent weeks.Biden's strong words towards Iran's leadership signal a shift in U.S. policy towards the country. The President has made it clear that his administration will not tolerate Iran's destabilizing actions in the region.The warning comes as Iran has taken steps to enrich uranium beyond the limits set by the 2015 nuclear deal, which the country has been violating since the United States withdrew from the agreement in 2018. The Biden administration has been working to reengage with Iran in an effort to revive the deal, but progress has been slow.Iran's leaders have dismissed Biden's comments as empty threats, but the President's warning serves as a stark reminder of the consequences of Iran's actions. As tensions continue to rise, the international community will be watching closely to see how both sides navigate this precarious situation.

The United States has surpassed 250,000 coronavirus deaths as new cases surge in many parts of the country.New York City on Wednesday announced the closure of its school system, the nation’s largest, with the city recording a seventh consecutive day with a COVID-19 positivity rate above 3%.“Public school buildings will be closed as of tomorrow, Thursday Nov. 19, out [of] an abundance of caution. We must fight back the second wave of COVID-19,” New York City Mayor Bill de Blasio wrote on Twitter.In-person school resumed for New York children between late September and early October, when the seven-day positivity rate was under 2%.Other major cities, including Boston and Detroit, have made recent moves to halt in-person classes for their schools.Across the United States there have been more than 11.5 million confirmed cases since the pandemic began.The current wave of infections is adding to that number at an increased rate with an average of nearly 160,000 new cases each day during the past week. That is about triple the number of new daily cases in the United States one month ago. More than 1,100 people are dying per day.Health care workers are dealing with the strain of a record number of hospitalized COVID-19 patients.The surge has pushed leaders in many states to reimpose certain restrictions in order to try to slow the spread of the virus.Among the latest, Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz announced Wednesday that all restaurants, bars and gyms would close for four weeks.

Relatives of the late singer Aretha Franklin say her funeral will be August 31 in her hometown of Detroit.There will be a two-day public viewing before the funeral at the Charles H. Wright Museum of African American History in Detroit, a publicist for the family, Gwendolyn Quinn, said, to allow fans to mourn.The funeral will take place at Greater Grace Temple, which seats about 4,000 people and will be limited to family, friends and invited guests, which are expected to include dignitaries and music royalty from around the world.Following the funeral, Franklin is to be buried with other members of her family at Woodlawn Cemetery.Franklin died Thursday at her home in Detroit at the age of 76 from pancreatic cancer.FILE - Aretha Franklin performs during the commemoration of the Elton John AIDS Foundation 25th year fall gala at the Cathedral of St. John the Divine in New York City, Nov. 7, 2017.The Grammy-winning vocalist was born in Memphis, Tennessee, but grew up in Detroit after moving there as a youngster with her family.Her talent was recognized at a young age as she played piano and sang hymns at the church where her father, C.L. Franklin, was a popular preacher.Franklin moved into pop and jazz music at age 18 on the Columbia Records label.

In a final push to win over voters, the leading candidates in France's upcoming presidential election gathered in Paris for a rally just days before the crucial vote. The atmosphere was charged with anticipation as the candidates delivered their closing arguments and made their final appeals to the French electorate.Incumbent President Emmanuel Macron, far-right candidate Marine Le Pen, centrist candidate Emmanuel Macron, and left-wing candidate Jean-Luc Mélenchon all addressed the crowds gathered in the capital city. Each candidate outlined their vision for the future of France and highlighted the key issues facing the country, from the economy to security to social welfare.The rally in Paris comes at a critical moment in the presidential campaign, with polls showing a tight race between Macron and Le Pen. The outcome of the election is uncertain, and the candidates are leaving no stone unturned in their efforts to sway undecided voters and mobilize their supporters.As the clock ticks down to election day, the eyes of the world are on France as the country prepares to choose its next leader. The stakes are high, and the candidates know that the future of France is in their hands. With just days to go until the vote, the race is still wide open, and anything could happen.

Despite intensive negotiations, key members of the World Trade Organization (WTO) have failed to reach an agreement on key issues related to the current trade round.The trade round, known as the Doha Development Agenda, aims to lower trade barriers and improve trading conditions for developing countries. However, disagreements over agriculture subsidies, market access for goods and services, and intellectual property rights have hindered progress in the negotiations.Major players such as the United States, European Union, China, and India have been unable to bridge their differences, leading to a stalemate in the talks. This lack of consensus has raised concerns about the future of the WTO and the ability of its members to make meaningful progress on global trade issues.The failure to reach an agreement comes at a critical time, as the COVID-19 pandemic has further disrupted global trade and economic growth. Many had hoped that a successful trade round could help stimulate economic recovery and support countries in their efforts to rebuild their economies post-pandemic.As negotiations continue, it remains to be seen whether key WTO members will be able to overcome their differences and reach a compromise that benefits all parties involved. In the meantime, the uncertainty surrounding the trade round underscores the challenges facing the global trading system in the years to come.

President Donald Trump is set to sign an executive order aimed at expanding offshore drilling in U.S. waters. The order, which is part of Trump's energy agenda to promote domestic energy production, will open up new areas for oil and gas exploration. This move is expected to be met with both support and criticism. Supporters argue that increased offshore drilling will boost the economy and create jobs, while critics raise concerns about the potential environmental impact of such activities.The order is also expected to roll back some of the regulations put in place by the Obama administration, which restricted offshore drilling in certain areas. Trump has long been a proponent of expanding domestic energy production and reducing regulations on the energy industry.The details of the executive order are still being finalized, but it is expected to have significant implications for the future of offshore drilling in the United States. Stay tuned for more updates on this developing story.

The medical aid agency Doctors Without Borders, known by its French acronym MSF, says an increase in the number of armed conflicts and troop movements in the eastern Democratic Republic of Congo are hampering its operations there. Cyril Bertrand, operational coordinator for MSF Belgium, tells VOA that rising violence and crime within the past few months have made it more and more difficult to get medical aid to those who need it.?Last week we faced harassment and two of our colleagues were kidnapped near Nyanzale," said Bertrand. "Before, by the end of 2011, we faced an incident as well in Masisi. At that time, one expatriate was injured by gunshots.?According to the group?s statement, released Thursday, the aid agency?s teams have been exposed to more than 15 acts of violence since last November, among the most serious being a raid last week on an MSF house in Baraka in South Kivu province, carried out by armed men in uniform.Typically, food, money, and mobile telephones are stolen, mostly to supply the military, says the group.Bertrand says it is the people living in North and South Kivu who bear the brunt of the violence?s consequences.?The teams have been evacuated from many places, because we cannot work in a safe situation," said Bertrand. "We had to evacuate five places since last week.?The group has suspended its activities in Nyanzale, reduced programs in Rutshuru, and recalled its team from the Butembo area.The statement says local populations are too afraid to come to clinics or even to cultivate in the fields, as people are commonly robbed, forced to carry armed men?s bags, and sometimes kidnapped.The latest volatility is due mainly to mass defections of former rebels from the Congolese army. The defecting soldiers are loyal to former rebel leader Bosco Ntaganda, who has been indicted by the International Criminal Court (ICC). Last month, the ICC convicted Ntaganda?

Relatives of Japanese citizens who were abducted by North Korea decades ago have been promised strong government support by Japanese Prime Minister Fumio Kishida. During a meeting with the families of the abductees, Kishida expressed his commitment to working towards the return of their loved ones and ensuring their well-being.The issue of Japanese citizens being abducted by North Korea in the 1970s and 1980s has been a long-standing and sensitive issue between the two countries. Japan has been seeking the return of its citizens and answers about their fate for years.Kishida assured the families that the Japanese government will continue to press North Korea on the issue through various diplomatic channels and will provide support to the families in their quest for the truth. The Prime Minister emphasized that resolving the abduction issue is a top priority for his administration.The families of the abductees welcomed the government's pledge of strong support and expressed their gratitude for the ongoing efforts to bring their loved ones back home. They have been tirelessly advocating for the return of their family members and hope that the recent promises of support will lead to a breakthrough in the long-standing issue.

In a landmark decision, the US Supreme Court has ruled that police must obtain a warrant in order to track an individual's location through their cellphone. This ruling, which was made in a 5-4 vote, strengthens the privacy rights of American citizens and sets a clear boundary on law enforcement's ability to conduct warrantless searches.The case, Carpenter v. United States, centered around Timothy Carpenter, who was convicted of multiple armed robberies based in part on location data obtained from his cellphone without a warrant. The Supreme Court's decision found that accessing this data constituted a violation of Carpenter's Fourth Amendment rights, which protect against unreasonable searches and seizures.Chief Justice John Roberts, who authored the majority opinion, emphasized the importance of safeguarding individuals' privacy in the digital age. He noted that the unprecedented amount of personal information stored on cellphones makes them particularly sensitive, and that accessing this data without a warrant is a violation of privacy rights.The dissenting justices argued that allowing law enforcement to track cellphone locations without a warrant is necessary for criminal investigations and public safety. However, the majority decision affirmed that the protection of privacy rights outweighs the potential benefits of warrantless cellphone tracking.This decision sets a significant precedent for the protection of digital privacy rights in the United States. Moving forward, law enforcement agencies will be required to obtain a warrant in order to track an individual's location through their cellphone, ensuring that Americans' privacy rights are upheld in the digital age.

Aid agencies operating in the Central African Republic (CAR) are facing a moral dilemma as they navigate the complexities of providing assistance in a conflict-ridden and politically unstable environment. The CAR has been plagued by violence and instability for years, with numerous armed groups vying for power and control over resources.Aid agencies are tasked with providing humanitarian assistance to those in need, including food, shelter, and medical care. However, the presence of armed groups and ongoing violence in the CAR has made it difficult for aid workers to reach those most in need. In some cases, aid agencies have been forced to negotiate with armed groups in order to access certain areas or deliver aid.This raises ethical concerns for aid agencies, as they must balance their humanitarian mandate with the potential risks of engaging with armed groups. Some critics argue that negotiating with armed groups legitimizes their power and could potentially put aid workers at risk. On the other hand, some aid agencies argue that they have no choice but to engage with armed groups in order to reach vulnerable populations and provide life-saving assistance.The moral dilemma faced by aid agencies in the CAR highlights the challenges of operating in complex and conflict-affected environments. As the situation in the CAR continues to evolve, aid agencies will need to carefully consider the ethical implications of their actions and decisions in order to effectively provide assistance to those in need.
A recent study published in the Journal of Nutrition has revealed that consuming processed tomatoes may significantly reduce the risk of prostate cancer in animals.

One day after the bloody clashes in southern Thailand that killed more than 100 people, grieving families have buried most of the dead and the Thai government is trying to determine who was behind the incident. Meanwhile, in the neighborhood where a standoff at a mosque led to the bloodiest clash, residents are bewildered and angry.The Krue Se mosque is a small, one-story brick building with a tin roof. More than 400-years-old, it sits on the outskirts of Pattani next to a busy highway.One day after the eight-hour siege in which 32 attackers were killed by Thai security forces, people have washed the blood from its tiled floor and cleaned the bronze plaque inscribed with Koranic sayings.They have not yet repaired the loudspeaker, so the unamplified voice of an elderly muezzin calls the faithful to prayer for the first time since the attack.About 100 local people have gathered to pray and inspect the damage to the old building. After prayers, some stay to discuss the event that has many of them still in shock.Mustafa Sabdam lives nearby and came running over when he heard the shooting.

In a significant development in the conflict in Aceh, a senior rebel leader has surrendered to authorities. The rebel, who goes by the name of Hasan, turned himself in to the Aceh authorities on Thursday.Hasan was a prominent figure in the Free Aceh Movement (GAM), which has been fighting for independence in the region for decades. His surrender is seen as a major blow to the rebel group and a step towards peace in the conflict-ridden province.Authorities have praised Hasan's decision to surrender and have promised to provide him with support as he transitions back into civilian life. This move is seen as a positive development in the ongoing efforts to end the violence and bloodshed in Aceh.The Indonesian government has been engaged in peace talks with GAM in an effort to bring an end to the long-standing conflict. Hasan's surrender is seen as a step towards reaching a peaceful resolution to the conflict and a glimmer of hope for the people of Aceh.The surrender of a senior rebel leader like Hasan is a significant development and a sign that progress is being made in the efforts to achieve peace in Aceh. It remains to be seen what impact this surrender will have on the overall situation in the province, but many are hopeful that it will bring about positive change and pave the way for a peaceful future for the people of Aceh.

Wild fires are burning out of control in the western Canadian province of British Columbia. Vast expanses of land have been scorched and thousands have been evacuated from their homes northeast of Vancouver.The situation for those fighting the fires is getting worse. Some of the largest fires, which have already caused widespread damage, appear to be growing in size. One of the larger blazes actually jumped over the North Thompson River, a major waterway that leads to the town of Kamloops.An extended summer drought, hot temperatures, and fierce winds continue to hamper firefighters. The weather forecast is calling for slightly cooler temperatures, but thundershowers could bring lightening strikes. It is suspected that half of the more than 350 fires currently burning around the province were started by lightening, the rest by humans.By Saturday night, approximately 75 houses, 150 outbuildings, and some businesses were destroyed around the small rural towns of McLure and Barriere.At least 8,500 people in total have been forced from their homes, with more evacuations pending.British Columbia Premier Gordon Campbell declared a province-wide state of emergency Saturday. Military personnel and firefighters from across Canada have been called in to assist local authorities.A fire in neighboring Washington state, which has been burning since the end of June, is also threatening to cross the Canadian-American border. Major fires are also burning in the states of Idaho and Montana.

MEXICO CITY - Veteran leftist Andres Manuel Lopez Obrador was sworn in as Mexican president on Saturday, promising a radical change of regime in a country struggling with gang violence, chronic poverty and corruption on the doorstep of the United States. Backed by a giant Mexican flag, the 65-year-old took the oath of office in Mexico's lower house of Congress, saying his administration would overturn what he called the disastrous legacy of decades of "neo-liberal" governments. "Starting now, transformation is under way, ordered and peaceful but at the same time radical, because we will end the corruption and impunity that impede Mexico's rebirth," he said. The first leftist to take office in Mexico in a generation also tried to reassure business after markets crashed since the July 1 election on worries about his policies, including the abrupt cancellation of a $13 billion airport project. Lopez Obrador reiterated that investments in the country of 130 million people would be safe, and he vowed to respect central bank independence. Saying his government would make savings by stopping losses from the public purse into the "sewer of corruption," he promised not to raise national debt or taxes. But in a reference to one of his heroes, former President Benito Juarez, who separated the church and the state in Mexico, Lopez Obrador promised his government would ensure a divide between economic and political power in the country.

Athletes participating in the upcoming Olympic Games in Tokyo have been asked to refrain from protesting at Olympic venues. The International Olympic Committee (IOC) issued a statement reminding athletes of Rule 50 in the Olympic Charter, which prohibits any form of protest, demonstration, or political, religious or racial propaganda at Olympic venues.

A group of protestors have occupied the Taiwan Parliament in an effort to demand transparency in the proposed trade deal with China. The demonstrators, who are mostly young students and activists, have been camping out in the parliament building for several days now.The trade deal in question is a controversial agreement that would further integrate Taiwan's economy with China's. Critics of the deal argue that it could compromise Taiwan's sovereignty and economic independence.

Malaysia has released the body of Kim Jong Nam, the half brother of North Korean leader Kim Jong Un, after a prolonged diplomatic dispute between the two countries. Kim Jong Nam was assassinated at Kuala Lumpur International Airport in 2017 by two women who smeared a toxic nerve agent on his face. North Korea has denied any involvement in the murder, while Malaysian authorities have accused North Korean agents of orchestrating the attack. The release of Kim Jong Nam's body comes after negotiations between Malaysia and North Korea, and the body will be flown to North Korea for burial. The resolution of this issue marks a rare moment of cooperation between the two countries, which have had strained relations in recent years.

The State Department says Taiwan's vice president, Annette Lu, will transit through the United States later this week on a trip to Central America. The trip is described as private but she will meet some members of the U.S. Congress.Though the United States has not had diplomatic relations with Taiwan since 1979, it has allowed leaders of the island's government to make transit stops as a courtesy and the State Department says Ms. Lu's trip later this week will be no exception.The Taiwanese vice president, sworn in for another four year term along with President Chen Shui-Bian last week, will arrive in Las Vegas Friday and spend a few days there before flying on to Central America. Her return plans call for a similar stopover in San Francisco starting June 9 before a return flight to Taipei.At a news briefing, State Department spokesman Richard Boucher said Ms. Lu's plans were approved on the same basis as similar transits, for reasons of the safety, comfort and convenience of the traveler. He said she would be greeted by staff members of the nominally-private institute that handles U.S. affairs in Taiwan and meet with some members of Congress. But he said the understanding is she will have no public events or statements."Our understanding is that she has no media or public activities, that this is a private stopover for the purposes of transit, and we would expect that that's the way it will be carried out," said Mr.

In a shocking incident in New York City, a grisly suicide took place yesterday in protest against pollution. The deceased, a 35-year-old environmental activist, was found hanging from a tree in a public park with a note pinned to his shirt. The note detailed his frustration with the lack of action being taken to address the city's pollution problem and called on authorities to prioritize environmental protection.The incident has sparked outrage and sorrow among the community, with many expressing condolences for the activist's family and friends. Some have also taken to social media to voice their concerns about the urgent need for stronger environmental regulations to combat pollution in the city.Authorities are investigating the circumstances surrounding the suicide and have urged the public to come forward with any information that may assist in the case. The tragic event serves as a stark reminder of the pressing need for action to protect the environment and prevent further tragedies like this from occurring.

Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu appeared headed for an unprecedented fifth term Wednesday after his main rival, Benny Gantz, conceded defeat in the country's tight parliamentary election.With nearly all the votes counted, Netanyahu's conservative Likud Party and Gantz's centrist Blue and White Party each had 35 seats.But Likud and its right-wing coalition partners were forecast to end up with a total of 65 seats in the 120-seat parliament, and President Reuven Rivlin will again ask Netanyahu to form a government.Final results are expected Thursday.After Netanyahu Win, Trump Confident of Middle East PeaceAs the last votes cast by Israeli soldiers were being counted late Wednesday, Gantz, the former military chief, was holding on to the slim hope his coalition could still win. But he said that "we respect the decision of the people."President Donald Trump tweeted that he had spoken with Netanyahu and had congratulated him "on a great and hard-fought win. The United States is with him and the people of Israel all the way."An Israeli media photograph of a Netanyahu victory celebration showed a man waving a Trump banner.Earlier, Netanyahu told his supporters Likud had achieved a "tremendous victory." He said that while his potential coalition would be a right-wing government, he pledged he would be "prime minister for all."His comments stood in contrast to his appeal to voters in the final days of campaigning, when he sought to solidify his conservative base, vowing to annex settlements in the occupied West Bank.

Russia has strongly criticized a proposal by some U.S. lawmakers to question the translator who was present during the summit between President Donald Trump and Russian President Vladimir Putin in Helsinki last year. The proposal comes as part of ongoing investigations into possible collusion between the Trump campaign and Russia during the 2016 U.S. presidential election.Russian officials have described the proposal as "absolutely absurd" and a violation of diplomatic norms. They argue that questioning the translator would undermine the confidentiality of diplomatic negotiations and set a dangerous precedent for future summits.The summit between Trump and Putin in Helsinki was highly controversial, with many criticizing Trump for his friendly demeanor towards Putin and his refusal to publicly confront him on issues such as election interference.

The World Health Organization (WHO) has issued a new recommendation urging all travelers to Angola to receive the yellow fever vaccination before their trip. This comes in response to a recent outbreak of the disease in the country, with over 2,000 confirmed cases and hundreds of deaths reported.Yellow fever is a viral disease spread by mosquitoes, and can be deadly if not treated promptly. The vaccination is highly effective in preventing the disease and is recommended for all travelers to regions where yellow fever is prevalent.The WHO is working closely with health authorities in Angola to control the outbreak and prevent further spread of the disease. They are also urging neighboring countries to increase surveillance and vaccination efforts to protect their populations.Travelers to Angola are advised to consult with their healthcare provider or travel clinic to receive the yellow fever vaccine at least 10 days before their trip. It is also important to take precautions to avoid mosquito bites, such as wearing long sleeves and pants, using insect repellent, and sleeping under mosquito nets.By following these recommendations, travelers can help protect themselves and others from the risk of yellow fever and contribute to the control of the outbreak in Angola. Stay informed and stay safe while traveling to areas with yellow fever risks.

Police and protesters have been engaged in a standoff in Taiwan's legislature after students stormed the building to demand the government scrap a trade deal with China. The protesters Wednesday knocked down a large metal gate as they entered the legislative chamber late Tuesday and were using chairs to keep out police. Authorities say several officers were slightly injured when they made a failed attempt to clear the chamber. The students say the deal will…

In a bid to address the global plastic pollution crisis, China has announced a ban on importing plastic waste. This move by the United Nations Environment Programme has been praised as a significant step towards promoting recycling and reducing the amount of plastic waste ending up in landfills and oceans.China has been a major importer of plastic waste for years, but with the country's recent ban, it is expected to drive countries around the world to find more sustainable solutions for their own plastic waste. This ban has already sparked a global conversation on the need for better waste management practices and increased recycling efforts.Many environmental advocates see this ban as a positive development that will encourage countries to invest more in recycling infrastructure and technology. With China's decision to no longer accept plastic waste, there is a sense of urgency to find alternative solutions to deal with the mounting plastic waste crisis.The UN Environment Programme has called on countries to take this opportunity to strengthen their recycling systems and invest in more sustainable waste management practices. By working together, it is possible to reduce the amount of plastic waste that ends up polluting our environment and threatening our wildlife.While China's plastic trash ban may initially pose challenges for countries that heavily relied on exporting their plastic waste, it also presents an opportunity for them to rethink their approach to waste management and prioritize recycling and sustainable practices. Ultimately, this ban serves as a reminder of the urgent need to address the global plastic pollution crisis and work towards a more sustainable future.

Pakistan is prohibiting the owner of an airline from leaving the country following the crash of one of its planes that killed 127 people.Interior Minister Rehman Malik said Farooq Bhoja has been barred from leaving Pakistan while aviation officials investigate Friday's crash that killed all on board. Witnesses say the Boeing 737 crashed during a thunderstorm near the capital's international airport, and that it may have already been on fire before it hit the ground. An airline official has blamed bad weather for the crash, but the cause has not been determined. The plane's flight data recording system - key to any investigation - has been recovered.At first light Saturday, soldiers and emergency workers were seen searching for bodies among the wreckage. Bhoja Air had stopped operating for more than a decade, but relaunched in March with several used Boeings - a practice aviation officials say is not uncommon. While the airline had been flying between Lahore and Karachi, Friday's doomed flight from Karachi marked the first time the airline had flown to Islamabad.

Refugees in the United States are experiencing heightened fear and confusion as the government prepares to deport thousands of individuals back to their home countries. With the recent changes in immigration policies and increased enforcement measures, many refugees are uncertain about their futures and the safety of their families.The fear of being separated from loved ones and sent back to dangerous or unstable conditions is causing immense stress and anxiety among the refugee community. Many are also unsure about their legal rights and options for seeking protection or asylum in the face of deportation orders.Human rights organizations and advocates are calling on the government to reconsider these deportations and to provide more support and resources for refugees who are facing removal. They argue that sending vulnerable individuals back to potentially life-threatening situations goes against the principles of humanitarianism and compassion.As the deportations loom, refugees are left with a sense of uncertainty and vulnerability. Many are turning to community organizations and legal aid services for guidance and assistance in navigating the complex immigration system.The fate of these refugees remains uncertain, but one thing is clear: the fear and confusion surrounding their potential deportations are causing immense suffering and insecurity within the refugee community.

Media reports in Honduras say a power-sharing government between ousted President Manuel Zelaya and interim leader Roberto Micheletti is expected to be in place by November 5. Honduran officials say they are confident Congress will approve the deal to reinstate Mr. Zelaya, just weeks ahead of the November 29 presidential election. Neither Mr. Zelaya nor Mr. Micheletti will run in the election. Authorities say a date for the congressional vote on the agreement has not yet been set. The breakthrough pact came late Thursday with Mr. Micheletti authorizing his negotiating team to sign the agreement, which he said "marks the beginning of the end" of the country's lengthy political crisis. The Honduran Supreme Court would need to authorize Congress to vote on whether to allow Mr. Zelaya to return to power and serve the remaining three months of his term.U.S. Secretary of State Hillary Clinton hailed the agreement as "historic." The Organization of American States issued a statement commending Mr. Zelaya and Mr. Micheletti for showing what it called flexibility and patriotic sentiment.U.N. Secretary-General Ban Ki-moon said he hopes Honduras is now on the path to restoration of democratic, constitutional rule.Mr. Zelaya was expelled from Honduras by troops June 28, with opponents accusing him of trying to illegally change the constitution to extend his term in office. The ousted leader secretly returned to the country in September and took refuge at the Brazilian Embassy, where he remains.

Zimbabwe's main opposition Movement for Democratic Change (MDC) is denying media speculation that it has reached a power-sharing agreement with President Robert Mugabe's ruling party. The opposition party says the latest talks on power sharing with the ruling ZANU-PF that resumed last Friday in South Africa did not reach an agreement. The MDC blames the ruling ZANU-PF party for speculating on reaching a power sharing deal.The peace talks are, however expected to continue despite a deadlock on the power sharing deal. Nelson Chamisa is the spokesman for Zimbabwe's main opposition MDC. From the capital, Harare he tells reporter Peter Clottey that although the party is committed to the talks, its stance on the power sharing deal remains the same."The reason why they (the MDC negotiating team) went to South Africa was to have bilateral consultations with the South African team as instructed by President Mbeki. At no time did we have a face-to-face meeting with the negotiators of ZANU-PF. It was basically to try and articulate our position and to check if there has been any change of circumstances or facts on the ground vis-à-vis the whole dialogue process. But as it is there hasn't been any change," Chamisa noted.He said the main bone of contention has been the issue of power sharing."We are deadlocked on key issues around the issue of power.

Anti-corruption activists in Ukraine welcomed the conviction of Paul Manafort on charges of tax evasion and bank fraud, saying they hope his trial will give fresh impetus to Ukrainian probes into politicians and oligarchs in Kyiv who paid millions of dollars to U.S. President Donald Trump's former campaign manager.Ukrainian prosecutors should ask for the evidence used by U.S. prosecutors in the trial in the state of Virginia for their stalled probes into political corruption in Ukraine, they say.Most of the 18 fraud charges Manafort faced — he was found guilty on eight of them — stemmed from his work as a political consultant for ousted Ukrainian president Viktor Yanukovych and his Party of Regions. Manafort masterminded the political comeback in Kyiv of Yanukovych in 2010, six years after Ukraine's pro-democracy Orange Revolution blocked him from taking office after a disputed election.“Manafort’s case is [an] important message for Ukrainian society to continue to fight for fair politics,” tweeted Serhiy Leshchenko, a lawmaker and former journalist, who helped expose secret cash payments channeled to Manafort from the Party of Regions between 2007 and 2012. According to U.S. prosecutors, Manafort received from his Ukrainian paymasters more than $60 million — money Leshchenko and anti-corruption campaigners say was stolen from public funds.

Christian churches in Sudan are calling for a seat at the table in the ongoing peace talks between the Sudanese government and rebel groups. The churches, representing various Christian denominations in the country, argue that their voices and perspectives are crucial in achieving a lasting peace agreement.Sudan has been plagued by civil war for decades, with the conflict primarily between the Muslim north and the Christian and animist south. The churches have been active in providing humanitarian assistance and promoting dialogue between the warring factions.The Sudanese government and rebel groups have been engaged in peace talks in Kenya, mediated by the Intergovernmental Authority on Development (IGAD). While progress has been made, the churches believe that their involvement is necessary to ensure that the interests of all Sudanese people, regardless of their religion, are represented in the negotiations.The churches are urging the parties involved in the peace talks to recognize the importance of their inclusion and to allow them to participate in the discussions. They believe that a comprehensive and inclusive peace agreement is the only way to end the cycle of violence and bring stability to Sudan.As the peace talks continue, the churches are hopeful that their voices will be heard and that they will be able to contribute to the building of a peaceful and prosperous future for all Sudanese people.

INDIO, CALIFORNIA - Hip hop duo Outkast reunited for the first time in eight years a the Coachella Valley Music and Arts Festival on Friday, and brought a taste of old school hip hop with them to the stage. Outkast, formed by Atlanta rappers Andre 3000 and Big Boi, kicked off the anticipated set with "B.O.B'' from the duo's 2000 album "Stankonia,'' before taking it back to the 1990s with singles such as ``ATLiens'' and ``Skew It on the Bar-B.'' "It's good to be back,'' Big Boi, real name Antwan Andre Patton, said on the Coachella stage against the backdrop of the Southern California desert lit up at night. Outkast last worked together on 2006 album "Idlewild,'' from which the rappers performed "Vibrate'' and "Bowtie.'' The duo were joined by soul singer Janelle Monae on the stage as they sang her hit "Tightrope,'' and then by rapper Future, who rose to fame in the 1990s hip hop scene. The rappers saved their best for last, bringing out their most mainstream hits "Ms Jackson,'' "The Way You Move'' and ``Hey Ya'' at the end, before the set was cut short at 1:00 a.m. (4:00 a.m. ET), inciting boos from the crowd and apologies from Andre 3000.

The work of Iranian-born artist Shirin Neshat, whose photographs, films and video installations deal with gender, politics and religion in the Islamic world, has been heralded by art critics and collected by major museums. Although Neshat has lived in the United States since the 1970s, her work has most often been focused on the lives of women in Iran, and more recently with the aftermath of the Arab Spring in Egypt. In 2010, a critic for The Huffington Post named her "Artist of the Decade," saying that Neshat's art reflects the struggle for human rights by "voiding stereotypes," revealing the "diversity within Islam and Iran." Neshat's 2009 film, Women Without Men, a magical-realist tale of women in Iran at the time of the 1953 coup, won the top directing award at the Venice Film Festival. She is equally well known for her photographic portraits, on which she inscribes verses of Persian poetry in tiny script. Her current show, Our House Is on Fire, is at New York's Robert Rauschenberg Foundation, which chose Neshat for its annual human rights commission to an individual artist. She used it to make portraits of poor, elderly people in Egypt. She conceived the project after the sudden death of the daughter of her long-time photographer, Larry Barns. “The concept of this exhibition is about the aftermath of the wonderful Arab Spring, which we hope is still continuing, but for the moment has had some catastrophic results,” Neshat said in an interview.

Renowned African satirist, John Kimani, has raised concerns over the recent wave of journalist sackings in Kenya. Kimani, known for his sharp commentary on political and social issues, spoke out against the increasing number of media professionals losing their jobs in the country.The journalist layoffs come amidst a backdrop of political tension and media crackdowns in Kenya. Many believe that the government is behind the recent wave of sackings, targeting reporters and news outlets that are critical of the current administration.Kimani warned that the silencing of journalists and media organizations could have a chilling effect on freedom of expression in Kenya. He called on the government to uphold press freedom and protect the rights of journalists to report without fear of reprisal.The international community has also expressed concern over the journalist sackings in Kenya, with many calling for a transparent and independent investigation into the reasons behind the layoffs. As the situation continues to unfold, Kimani and other activists are vowing to continue speaking out against censorship and threats to media freedom in the country.

Britain can extradite jailed radical-Muslim preacher Abu Hamza al-Masri and four other alleged terrorists to the United States, according to a ruling by the European Court of Human Rights.Abu Hamza, a preacher who is serving a seven-year sentence in Britain, is wanted in the United States on 11 charges, including inciting racial hatred and conspiring to set up a jihad training camp in the United States.European Court official Claire Ovey spoke to journalists in Strasbourg."This is not a final judgment so we have to wait for three-months to see if it will go to the Grand Chamber or not," said Ovey. "If this judgment does become final then there will be no bar to Abu Hamza being extradited to the United States.?The European Court had been considering the cases of six people who were indicted in the United States on terror charges.The court delayed ruling on one of the six, who suffers from mental health issues, but said the other five men can be extradited to the United States.The defendants had argued the conditions they might face at a high security prison in the United States could expose them to ?torture or inhuman or degrading treatment or punishment," in breach of European human-rights laws. But the court ruled Tuesday the defendants would not face disproportionate sentences in the United States.Members of Britain?s Cabinet welcomed the decision. Prime Minister David Cameron said he was ?pleased with the news.?

ISLAMABAD - A new nationwide survey of public opinion in Afghanistan reports 58 percent of Afghans say the country is headed in the wrong direction.The San Francisco-based Asia Foundation released its annual survey Tuesday in Kabul, saying that overall optimism has decreased across all Afghan regions.The most frequently cited reason is insecurity (45 percent, up six percentage points from 2014), followed by unemployment, corruption in general, struggling economy and bad government.The survey shows that 37 percent of respondents nationwide believe their country is moving in the right direction, down from 55 percent in 2014.“This year, we see a sharp decrease in the levels of optimism among Afghans. This sharp decrease is the lowest percentage recorded in the 10 years of the survey,” an Asia Foundation official, Sayed Masood Sadat, told reporters in Kabul.The survey polled more than 9,500 Afghan men and women representing 14 ethnic groups across all 34 provinces. The poll had a 1.6 percent margin of error.FILE - Mullah Mohammed Rasool, the newly-elected leader of a breakaway faction of the Taliban, speaks during a gathering in Farah province, Afghanistan.Taliban reconciliation It says that around 62 percent Afghans believe the government’s efforts to reconcile with the Taliban and other armed opposition groups will help stabilize Afghanistan, showing a significant decrease from 73 percent in 2014.The Taliban, taking advantage of last year's withdrawal of NATO’s combat mission, has escalated and expanded its insurgent activities to more areas of Afghanistan than at any point in time since 2001.

The recent catastrophic accident at Russia's largest hydroelectric plant has reminded leaders in Moscow of the urgent need for inspections and repairs of the country's mostly Soviet-era infrastructure. Long before the deadly accident in Siberia in August, Russia's Emergency Situations Ministry had warned that the dam at the power plant was in a dangerous state of neglect, yet little was done over the next 10 years to prevent its tragic and costly failure. The accident at the Sayano-Shushenskaya hydroelectric plant highlighted the state of decay in Russia's infrastructure, and the government's faltering attempts to deal with the problem. Russian media found that authorities had warned of unsafe conditions more than a decade before a breach in the dam triggered calamitous floods that killed 75 people. Another more general warning about the country's infrastructure came in 2005, when the Emergency Situations Ministry said more than 60 percent of all water pipes, sewage, heating and electricity networks across Russia needed replacement urgently. The ministry predicted that aging Soviet-built facilities would be a major factor in the coming years' technological and industrial accidents."The average age of capital equipment in Russia is more than 20 years, so to a significant degree this is still Soviet-era capital equipment in Russia's industry, whereas in the States or in western Europe that would range from 10 to 15 years," says Yaroslav Lissovolik, who is chief economist for Deutsche Bank in Moscow. "I think Russia did not have enough time. Russia started its modernization effort and the effort of upgrading infrastructure in earnest in 2006, and it simply just didn't have the time.

The United Nations has emphasized the importance of creating the right conditions for credible elections to take place in the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC). The country has a history of political instability and unrest during election periods, and the UN is calling for measures to ensure a fair and transparent electoral process.UN spokespersons have expressed concerns about the current political climate in the DRC, citing reports of voter suppression, intimidation, and violence against political opponents. They have called on the government to uphold democratic principles and respect the rights of all citizens to participate in the electoral process without fear of reprisal.In order to achieve credible elections, the UN is urging the government to address issues such as voter registration, access to polling stations, and the independence of electoral institutions. The international community is also being called upon to support efforts to promote free and fair elections in the DRC.With the presidential election scheduled to take place in the near future, the UN is stressing the need for urgent action to create an environment conducive to a peaceful and democratic electoral process. Failure to do so could result in further unrest and instability in the country, which has already experienced significant challenges in its transition to democracy.

Dozens of suspected Uighurs from China are currently in a state of limbo in Thailand, as they await a decision on their asylum claims. The group of 60 individuals, including women and children, were detained in Thailand in 2014 and have since been stuck in a legal and bureaucratic maze.The Uighurs, a Turkic-speaking Muslim minority group, have faced persecution and discrimination in China, leading many to flee the country in search of safety. However, their journey to find refuge has been met with numerous challenges and roadblocks.Thailand, not being a signatory to the United Nations Refugee Convention, does not have a formal process for granting asylum to refugees. As a result, the fate of the suspected Uighurs remains uncertain as they navigate through the complexities of the legal system.Human rights organizations have called for the Thai government to uphold its international obligations and provide protection to the Uighurs. They argue that sending the group back to China could put their lives in danger and violate their rights.The situation highlights the difficulties faced by refugees and asylum seekers around the world, who are often caught in legal and bureaucratic limbo with no clear path to safety. As the suspected Uighurs in Thailand continue to wait for a resolution to their plight, their future remains uncertain and precarious.

Chinese stocks will be included for the first time in a leading U.S. index of emerging market shares.The New York-based index giant MSCI said Tuesday that it would add 222 Chinese A shares beginning next year."International investors have embraced the positive changes in the accessibility of the China A shares market over the last few years, and now all conditions are set for MSCI to proceed with the first step of the inclusion," Remy Briand, MSCI managing director and chairman of the MSCI Index Policy Committee, said in a release.MSCI's decision to give the Chinese shares the green light represents a victory for the Chinese government, which has long sought MSCI inclusion because it could help establish Shanghai and Shenzhen as global financial centers.MSCI has in the past cited obstacles such as China's restrictions on market access and on moving capital in and out of the country. Prior to Tuesday's decision, it had excluded Chinese shares for three years in a row."

LONDON - Paul McCartney has written a children's book called Hey Grandude!, a name the 76-year-old former member of The Beatles says came from one of his grandchildren."I've got eight grandchildren and they're all beautiful and one day one of them said to me 'Hey Grandude!' I said 'What?' and I thought, I kind of like that," McCartney said in a video he posted Thursday on Twitter."From then on, I was kind of known as Grandude."The picture book tells the story of a grandfather with four grandchildren and is illustrated by Toronto-based artist Kathryn Durst."He calls them 'Chillers.' They love him and they go on adventures with him and he's kind of magical," McCartney said.McCartney is currently on his "Freshen Up" world tour featuring songs from his latest album, "Egypt Station."The book will be published by Penguin Random House UK and is available for pre-order before it goes on sale in Sept. 2019.

An Amish man from Lancaster County, Pennsylvania has been making headlines for his unique choice of attire during marathons. Instead of the usual running gear, Jacob Miller has been seen sporting traditional Amish garb, including a straw hat, suspenders, and a long-sleeved button-up shirt.Despite the unconventional outfit, Miller has been able to successfully complete multiple marathons, even achieving personal best times. When asked about his choice of clothing, Miller explained that he wanted to showcase his Amish heritage and stand out from the crowd.While some may find his attire to be a hindrance, Miller insists that the loose-fitting clothing actually helps him feel more comfortable and allows for better airflow during long runs. He has become a familiar face at local races and has garnered a following of supporters who cheer him on as he crosses the finish line.Miller's dedication to both his Amish roots and his passion for running has inspired many in the community. He hopes to continue participating in marathons and spreading awareness about the Amish way of life through his unique running attire.

As the anniversary of the Tiananmen Square Massacre approaches, residents of Hong Kong are expressing concern about the future of the annual vigil held in Victoria Park to commemorate the tragic event. The vigil has been a tradition in Hong Kong for over three decades, serving as a symbol of the city's dedication to preserving the memory of the pro-democracy movement in China.However, with the recent implementation of the National Security Law in Hong Kong, many fear that the vigil may be banned by authorities. The law, which was enacted by the Chinese government last year, criminalizes acts of secession, subversion, terrorism, and collusion with foreign forces.Despite the risks, organizers of the vigil have vowed to continue with their plans for this year's event. They believe it is essential to honor the memory of those who lost their lives during the massacre and to show solidarity with pro-democracy activists in China.The future of the Tiananmen Square vigil remains uncertain, but residents of Hong Kong are determined to ensure that the tradition is upheld for years to come. They see the vigil as a vital form of resistance against authoritarianism and a way to keep the memory of the massacre alive in the hearts of all who attend.

China and Russia have announced their commitment to quadruple trade between the two countries by the end of this decade. Chinese President Xi Jinping and Russian President Vladimir Putin made the announcement during a virtual meeting on Friday.The two leaders expressed their mutual desire to strengthen economic ties and cooperation, emphasizing the importance of their strategic partnership. Currently, China is Russia's largest trading partner, with bilateral trade reaching over $110 billion in 2020.The ambitious goal to quadruple trade signifies a deepening of economic relations between the two countries, despite geopolitical tensions with the West. The leaders discussed ways to enhance cooperation in various sectors, including energy, technology, and infrastructure.Both China and Russia see immense potential for growth in their economic partnership and are committed to further expanding their trade volume in the coming years. The announcement is seen as a significant development in the evolving relationship between the two major powers.

Tesla Inc Chief Executive Officer Elon Musk acknowledged on Friday that it was "foolish" of him to snub analysts on a conference call earlier in the week, but further needled Wall Street with a series of accusatory tweets.In a post-earnings call on Wednesday, Musk refused to answer questions from analysts on the electric vehicle maker's capital requirements, saying "boring, bonehead questions are not cool," before turning questions over to a little known investor who runs HyperChange, a YouTube investment channel.The outspoken performance shocked many analysts, sparked a fall in Tesla's share price and led some to question whether Musk's behavior could risk the company's ability to raise capital.In early-morning tweets on Friday, Musk said the two analysts he cut off — RBC Capital Markets' Joseph Spak and Bernstein's Toni Sacconaghi — "were trying to justify their Tesla short thesis."'Shorting' means they were betting the stock would fall, but the two have 'hold' or 'neutral' ratings on the stock, according to Thomson Reuters data. "I should have answered their questions live. It was foolish of me to ignore them," Musk tweeted.The two analysts were not immediately available for a comment.The spat comes at a crunch time for Tesla, when it is struggling to ramp up production of its Model 3 sedan, on which its profitability depends.

Pakistan's fragile coalition government has made little progress on the key issue that swept them to power in February's elections: reinstating judges who were thrown out of office by President Musharraf last November. In an interview with VOA's Barry Newhouse in Lahore, Pakistan Muslim League-N party leader and former prime minister Nawaz Sharif said reinstating the judges is directly linked to resolving the country's other problems.In the February elections, Nawaz Sharif's PML-N party won the second-largest number of seats in the national assembly, largely because of Mr. Sharif's strong opposition to President Musharraf, whom he continues to insist should step down."People want to see the change that they voted for," he said. "Of course the first change they wanted to see and still want to see is to have a new president of this country.
